The drug’s manufacturer has not yet said how much it would cost if approved by the F.D.A. as a preventive medication, not that it will cost $42,000 a year.
match_headline: Supreme Court Wrestles With Challenge to Affordable Care Act Over Free Preventive Care

text: An article on April 16 about the actor, musician and writer Whitney White misstated the title of a play directed by White. It is “Our Dear Dead Drug Lord,” not “Our Dear Drug Lord.”
match_headline: Onstage and Off, Whitney White Is Everywhere This Spring

text: An article on Tuesday about the health benefits of asparagus misstated the title of Vanessa da Silva and her affiliation with the University of Arizona. She is a dietitian and extension specialist at the University of Arizona, but is not a diabetes educator affiliated with the Sarver Heart Center.
match_headline: How Healthy Is Asparagus?

text: An article on Sunday about an Australian trademark lawsuit between Katie Perry, an Australian designer with a namesake fashion label, and the pop musician Katy Perry misidentified the intellectual property dispute that had prevented Burger King from operating under that name in Australia. Another restaurant originally owned the trademark for the name, not the copyright.
match_headline: Katie Perry Is Suing Katy Perry in Australia. Wait, What?

text: An article on Wednesday about a federal judge’s decision that blocked the Trump administration from dismantling Voice of America, a government-funded news organization that President Trump has accused of being biased against him, misstated when Mr. Trump signed an executive order seeking to gut the U.S. Agency for Global Media. It was March 14, not March 15.
match_headline: Judge Blocks Trump Effort to Dismantle Voice of America

text: An article on Thursday about President Trump’s proposal to Ukraine that essentially grants Russia all the territory it has gained in the war, while offering Kyiv only vague security assurances misstated the number of years that the Crimean Peninsula was considered part of Russia. It was more than 150 years, not hundreds of years.
match_headline: Trump Pressures Ukraine to Accept a Peace Plan That Sharply Favors Russia

text: An article on Thursday about Harvey Weinstein’s retrial in Manhattan misstated Susan Hoffinger’s title. She is the senior counsel to the district attorney, not chief of the investigations division.
match_headline: Weinstein’s Retrial Begins, Testing Legacy of #MeToo Movement

text: An article on Friday about the U.S. Naval Academy’s efforts to anticipate the desires of an unpredictable presidential administration misidentified one of the institutions criticizing the invitation of Ruth Ben-Ghiat to speak at the U.S. Naval Academy. It was the publication The Federalist, not the legal group the Federalist Society.
match_headline: From Book Bans to Canceled Lectures, the Naval Academy Is Bending to Trump

text: An article on Friday about a lawsuit filed by an assortment of advocacy groups aimed at stopping the Department of Homeland Security from permanently shuttering its internal oversight divisions after the Trump administration fired critical staff members misstated where the lawsuit was filed. It is Washington, D.C., not New York.
match_headline: Lawsuit Aims to Reverse Firings at Internal Oversight Offices Within D.H.S.

text: An article this weekend on Page 8 about the Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History coming under attack by the Trump administration for the diversity it represents misidentifies part of the title of Marie Madison-Patton, MOCAD’s co-director. She is also the chief operating officer, not the chief financial officer. The article also describes incorrectly the role of the Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture in the “Code Switch” exhibition. The exhibition was first held at the center, not in partnership with it; a second part will open at MOCAD on May 2. This article also includes an outdated description of The Kitchen. Initially an artists collective, it is now an arts institution. This article also misstates the participation of community groups at MOCAD during the “Gun Violence Memorial Project” exhibit. The groups will not be providing antidrug and anti-violence information as part of the exhibit.
match_headline: Detroit Art Institutions Resist Political Challenges to Diversity

text: An article this weekend on Page 26 about the artist Ann Craven misspells the given name of the curator at the Farnsworth Art Museum. It is Jaime DeSimone, not Jamie. This article also misstates who would be organizing rotating displays at the show at the Bowdoin College Museum of Art. They will be organized by three curators, not by students and prominent figures in the Maine art community.
match_headline: A Painter Famed for Recreating What She Lost, in the Spotlight
match_authors: Winnie Au For The New York Times

text: An article this weekend on Page 34 about Brazilian artist Adriana Varejão’s first solo museum exhibit in New York misstates the name of the earliest plate made by Varejão for her show at the Hispanic Society Museum and Library. It is “Mucura,” not “Mucara.”
match_headline: Plates Full of Beauty and History in Upper Manhattan

text: An article this weekend on Page 43 about a space photography exhibition at the American Museum of Natural History in Manhattan reverses the descriptions of two images of a barred spiral galaxy that are displayed one above the other in the exhibition. The image on top, from the Hubble Space Telescope, looks like a swirl of light, not a circle of fire, and the bottom image, from the James Webb Space Telescope, resembles a circle of fire, not a swirl of light.
match_headline: Glimpses of the Final Frontier at the American Museum of Natural History

text: An article this weekend on Page 44 about younger museum curators working to broaden audiences while focusing on populations and cultures that were previously ignored misstates the title of Nicola Lees at the Aspen Museum. She is the artistic director and chief executive, not the director.
match_headline: New Voices Help Museums Tell New (or Forgotten) Stories

text: A review this weekend on Page 21 of “On Air: The Triumph and Tumult of NPR,” by Steve Oney, misstates the reviewer’s position at the Columbia Journalism Review. The reviewer, Sewell Chan, is the publication’s former executive editor.
ref_headline: On Air: The Triumph and Tumult of NPR,

text: A picture caption with an article about President Trump’s meeting with Ukraine’s president, Volodymyr Zelensky, misstated the day that the photo of Ukrainian troops in Donetsk was taken. It was taken on Friday, April 18, not April 25.
match_headline: I’m in Denmark’s Parliament. Here’s Why America Has Us So Stunned.

text: An article on Sunday about the third round of talks between Iran and the United States over Iran’s nuclear activities misstated when Iran pulled away from obligations under a nuclear agreement. It was about a year after President Trump exited the pact in 2018, not a year after the deal was reached.
match_headline: Trump Says U.S. Could Lead Military Action Against Iran if Talks Collapse

text: An article on Sunday about President Trump’s executive order that aims to permit, for the first time, industrial mining of the seabed for minerals misstated the given name of a scientist at the Bigelow Laboratory for Ocean Sciences. She is Beth Orcutt, not Bethany.
match_headline: The Trump Administration Wants Seafloor Mining. What Does That Mean?

text: An article on April 22 about China’s efforts to replace its reliance on American soybeans misstated how the Chinese food company Cofco has used $500 million in investments in Brazil. The company has invested the money in various projects in Brazil, not just in the port terminal in Santos, Brazil. The article also misstated the name of a Brazilian trade group. It is the Brazilian Association of Vegetable Oil Industries, not Vegetable Oil Producers.
match_headline: China Is Finding Ways to Replace American Farmers

text: An article on Sunday about the various exhibitions and events celebrating the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence used an incorrect title for Noelle Trent. She is the president and chief executive officer of the Museum of African American History in Boston and Nantucket, Mass., not the director.
match_headline: Museums Tell Stories of American Independence

text: An article on Sunday about one of the finest private art collections in the world owned by Henry Clay Frick provided an incorrect location for the Carnegie plant that was the site of a strike in 1892. It was located in Homestead, Pa., not Hempstead, Pa.
match_headline: He Built the Frick Collection With Passion, Patience and Bargaining

text: An article on Sunday about a school lunch exhibition at the Science History Institute in Philadelphia misstated the number of students who received a free or reduced-price lunch on an average school day. According to the Food Research & Action Center’s 2024 report, 19.7 million students received a free or reduced-price lunch, not 23.6 million. (About 23.6 million students were enrolled in high-poverty districts that qualify for free lunches for all.)
match_headline: If You Think the School Lunch Battle is New — Go to Philadelphia

text: An obituary on Monday about Alexis Herman, a Democratic Party insider who went on to become the first Black secretary of labor, misstated the name of one of Ms. Herman’s survivors, a stepson. He is Charles J. Franklin, not Charles Franklin Jr.
match_headline: Alexis Herman, First Black Secretary of Labor, Is Dead at 77

text: An article on Tuesday about Senator Josh Hawley of Missouri misstated which abortion-access case Senator Hawley’s wife, Erin Morrow Hawley, argued before the Supreme Court. She argued a case that unsuccessfully sought to curtail access to mifepristone, a medication used in abortions, not the case that overturned Roe v. Wade.
match_headline: Josh Hawley and the Republican Populists, at War With Their Party

text: An article on Tuesday about National Guard troops being deployed to Albuquerque next month because of violent crime and fentanyl use misstated the role that those troops might play in the transportation of detainees to jail. They are expected to provide administrative support only.
match_headline: Albuquerque Has a Crime Problem. Is the National Guard the Answer?

text: An article on Wednesday about the case heard by the Supreme Court on Tuesday regarding the botched F.B.I. operation in 2017, in which they raided the wrong house, misspelled the given name of a Supreme Court justice. He is Neil M. Gorsuch, not Neal.
match_headline: Supreme Court Considers Suit Over F.B.I.’s Raid of the Wrong House

text: An article on Monday about what to watch on television this week misidentified the actor who plays Jack in “The Four Seasons.” He is Will Forte, not Steve Carell. Carell plays a different character in the series.
match_headline: ‘The Four Seasons,’ Plus 7 Things to Watch on TV this Week

text: An article on April 25 about new research that aims to assign specific responsibility to polluters misstated the extent of flooding caused when the Winooski River spilled its banks in 2023. While floodwaters entered some government buildings, they did not pour onto the floor of the State House.
match_headline: New Study Could Bolster Climate Laws to Make Polluters Pay

text: An article on Thursday about a potential train engineer strike that would affect New Jersey Transit customers misstated the timing of the impending strike. It would take place later next month, not later this month.
match_headline: As Strike Looms, N.J. Transit Riders Are Warned to Prepare for ‘Chaos’

text: An article on Friday about the massacre of 26 people in Kashmir misstated the number of terrorism-related deaths in Indian-administered Kashmir in recent years. They have generally remained above 100, not declined to “no more than a few dozen.”
ref_headline: no more than a few dozen.
match_headline: Kashmir Is a Wonderland. An Attack Shows It Is Also a Cradle of Despair.

text: An article on Friday about fear and confusion gripping a community of Asian refugees as the Trump administration cracks down on immigration misstated the number of Bhutanese people living in refugee camps in Nepal. There are about 6,300 people, not 1,000 people.
match_headline: U.S. Deported Bhutanese Who Were Here Legally. They Are Now Stateless.

text: An article on Wednesday about Penske Media and its shake-up of the leadership of the South by Southwest festival misstated how it gained its control of South by Southwest. Two years ago, Penske, which owned a 50 percent share of the company with MRC, gained control of SXSW by buying another 1 percent; it did not purchase the additional 1 percent with MRC.
match_headline: Penske Shakes Up Leadership of South by Southwest

text: An article on Thursday about Mohsen Mahdawi, an organizer of the pro-Palestinian movement at Columbia University who was freed from federal custody last week, misstated with whom Gabriella Ramirez is acquainted. Ms. Ramirez knows Mahmoud Khalil, not Mr. Mahdawi.
match_headline: Columbia Student Who Was Arrested at Citizenship Interview Is Freed

text: An article on Sunday about the issues cardinals will debate to determine who becomes the next pope misstated the timing of a procession in a picture caption. It was three days before the funeral, not during the funeral itself.
match_headline: These Contentious Issues Could Determine Who Becomes the Next Pope

text: A picture caption with an article on Sunday about Ghana’s national cathedral project that has become a symbol of economic mismanagement misidentified which London structure the Ghana project was styled after. As the article correctly stated, it is Westminster Abbey, not Westminster Cathedral.
match_headline: Ghana Wanted a Cathedral. It Got an ‘Expensive Hole’ Instead.

text: An article on Sunday about Warren Buffett’s plans to step down as the head of Berkshire Hathaway by the end of the year misstated the year that Berkshire Hathaway bought Greg Abel’s energy company, now called Berkshire Hathaway Energy. The transaction closed in 2000, not 1999.
match_headline: Warren Buffett Says He Plans to Step Down as Head of Berkshire

text: An article on Saturday about the singer-songwriter Gigi Perez misspelled an artist’s surname. She is Hayley Kiyoko, not Kyioko.
match_headline: What Comes After Trauma and a TikTok Hit? Gigi Perez Is Finding Out.

text: An obituary on April 27 about the keyboardist and studio operator David Briggs referred incorrectly to Danor Music, the publishing company he founded with Norbert Putnam. It did not publish the songs “Higher Love” and “Didn’t We Almost Have It All”; those songs were published by Willin’ David Music, a company Mr. Briggs founded later with the songwriter Will Jennings. The obituary also misspelled part of the name of a recording studio Mr. Briggs and Mr. Putnam owned. It was Quadrafonic Sound, not Quadraphonic.
match_headline: David Briggs, a Music Force in Alabama and Nashville, Dies at 82

text: An obituary on Saturday about the singer and songwriter Jill Sobule, using information from her family, misstated when in 1959 she was born. It was Jan. 16, not Jan. 29.
match_headline: Jill Sobule, Who Sang the Original ‘I Kissed a Girl,’ Dies at 66

text: An article on Monday about Dr. Alan Garber, the president of Harvard who is pushing back against President Trump’s efforts to strip Harvard of research funding and its nonprofit tax status while trying to remake campus culture, referred imprecisely to Caleb Thompson at Harvard. He is one of two undergraduate student body presidents, not the only one.
match_headline: Harvard’s President Is Fighting Trump. He Also Agrees With Him.

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Tuesday about reports that the Sudanese paramilitary forces struck civilian targets in Sunday’s attack misstated the year when a photo of Brig. Gen. Nabil Abdullah was taken. It was from 2023, not last month.
match_headline: Sudan’s Army Says Paramilitary Forces Struck Civilian Targets

text: A chart with an article on March 10 about unfairness in the U.S. organ transplant system misstated organ discard rates. The discard rate was 21 percent in 2024, not 22 percent.
match_headline: Solar Energy, Criticized by Trump, Claims Big U.S. Gain in 2024

text: An article on Wednesday about U.S.A. Fencing finding itself at the center of a debate about inclusivity and transgender athletes misidentified the school on Staten Island that Red Sullivan had competed for until the N.C.A.A. ban. It is Wagner College, not Wagner University.
match_headline: A Fencing Match, a Viral Video and a Hearing Before Congress

text: An article on Monday about the uncertain future of the house in Folly Beach, S.C., where George Gershwin worked on the opera “Porgy and Bess” misstated the given name of the mayor of Folly Beach. He is Tim Goodwin, not Tom.
match_headline: Opera History Was Made in This House. Its Future Is Uncertain.

text: An article on Wednesday about the European artists Jeanne-Claude and Christo misstated the name of the museum in Fort Lauderdale where an exhibition of the highlights of “Surrounded Islands” opened in February. It is NSU Art Museum Fort Lauderdale, not NSU Fort Lauderdale Art Museum.
match_headline: Christo and Jeanne-Claude, Legendary Artists, Are Back in Spirit

text: An article on Monday about Bill Mott, the horse trainer of the Kentucky Derby-winning horse Sovereignty, misstated Michael Banahan’s ownership of the horse. Sovereignty is owned by a group, Godolphin, not by Banahan. Banahan, a member of Godolphin, was the group’s representative at the Kentucky Derby.
match_headline: Against the Odds, He Made Thoroughbred Racing a Viable Business

text: An article on Wednesday about Lencia Kebede, who made history by becoming the first Black actor to play the role of Elphaba full time in the Broadway company of “Wicked,” misspelled a character’s name in “Sister Act.” She is Deloris, not Dolores.
match_headline: An Actress of Many Passions, Now Making History in ‘Wicked’

text: A picture caption with an obituary on Thursday about the golfer Jim Dent misstated Dent’s accomplishments on the PGA Tour. As one of the few Black golfers on the Tour in his day or now, Dent won $564,809; he did not win his first tournament in 1979 followed by 11 more between 1982 and 1986. Those tournaments were won by Calvin Peete.
match_headline: Jim Dent, Long-Driving Golfer on PGA and Senior Tours, Dies at 85

text: An article on Friday about five New York art fairs to visit misidentified the founders of Esther. It was founded by Olga Temnikova and Margot Samel, not the gallery Temnikova & Kasela .
match_headline: 5 (More) Art Fairs to Welcome Spring

text: An article this weekend on Page 12 about the Netflix show “Forever,” a new adaptation of Judy Blume’s 1975 novel, misidentified the movie for which Regina King won an Oscar. It was “If Beale Street Could Talk,” not “If These Walls Could Talk.”
match_headline: ‘Forever’ Explores the Timelessness of Teen Romance (and Sex)

text: An article on Saturday about Laura Loomer’s vocal criticism of President Trump’s selection of Dr. Casey Means as his next surgeon general misstated the status of Dr. Means’s medical license. While she holds an inactive medical license, it is current.
match_headline: Far-Right Activist Targets Trump’s Surgeon General Pick

text: An article on Sunday about an energetic group of educators, parents and researchers advancing an ambitious agenda for learning despite the lack of national direction misstated the title of the book George W. Bush read in a second-grade classroom. It was “The Pet Goat,” not “My Pet Goat.”
match_headline: Has America Given Up on Children’s Learning?

text: An article on Saturday about trade negotiation talks between the United States and China in Geneva misstated how many Americans have died after using the drug fentanyl. It is tens of thousands of Americans, not millions.
match_headline: U.S. and China End First Day of High-Stakes Economic Talks

text: Because of an editing error, an article on May 8 about efforts by the Smithsonian’s National Museum of African American History and Culture to grapple with an executive order from President Trump misstated the role of the Association of African American Museums in a rally to protect the museum. The association supported the rally, but did not help to organize it.
match_headline: National African American Museum Faces Uncertainty Without Its Leader

text: An article on Sunday about Fabrizio Brienza, who is among a handful of gatekeepers who decide which people are allowed inside various nightclubs and lounges for drinking and dancing, misidentified the man wearing Converse sneakers and standing in line in front of Paul’s in the early morning. His name is not Cameron Gucker.
match_headline: If Your Vibe Is Right, He Might Let You Into the Club

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about a project by Allan Wexler and Michael Yarinsky to update “The Futurist Cookbook” misstated the employment status of Natasha Pickowicz. She is no longer a pastry chef at the restaurant Altro Paradiso. The article also misidentified the location of Jane Lombard Gallery, where Mr. Wexler recently exhibited. It is in TriBeCa, not SoHo.
match_headline: Updating ‘The Futurist Cookbook,’ One Meal at a Time

text: An article on Sunday about the ways that supporters of President Trump have viewed his first 100 days in office misstated the location where Emily Haraldsson, a tech worker, resides. She lives in Jefferson County, Ky. — not Lexington County, Ky., which does not exist.
match_headline: For Trump Supporters, an ‘Exciting’ First 100 Days

text: An article on Sunday about a brass bracelet that was given to a Times journalist while he was covering the Vietnam War misstated the year Ralph Blumenthal began covering the war for The Times. It was 1969, not 1968.
match_headline: A Brass Bracelet With Both History and Meaning

text: An article on Tuesday about criticism by Jewish environmental activists of Lee Zeldin’s role in weakening rules designed to limit pollution and global warming described incorrectly the connection of Mr. Zeldin’s grandfather, Abraham Jacob Zeldin, to the Farmingdale Jewish Center on Long Island. While Abraham Jacob Zeldin helped to oversee the conversion of the building into a synagogue, he was not the founder of the center. The article also referred incorrectly to Abraham Jacob Zeldin’s occupation. He was not a rabbi.
match_headline: A Jewish Celebration at the E.P.A. Also Has Some Jewish Critics

text: Because of an editing error, an obituary on Tuesday about Johnny Rodriguez, the Mexican American country music star, misstated the status of Mr. Rodriguez’s marriage to Debbie McNeely at the time of his death. They were still married, according to his daughter; they were not divorced.
match_headline: Johnny Rodriguez, Country Music Star, Dies at 73

text: An obituary on Tuesday about William H. Luers, a longtime diplomat who later became president of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, misidentified the president with whom Vaclav Havel, the president of Czechoslovakia, met in the White House in 1990 when Mr. Luers arranged for Mr. Havel to visit the museum. It was George H.W. Bush, not George W. Bush.
match_headline: William H. Luers, Diplomat Who Backed Czech Dissident Leader, Dies at 95

text: An article on Friday about the Trump administration’s approach to South Africa, relying on the account of a congressional official, referred incorrectly to the participation of Dan Dunham, an official at the National Security Council, at a meeting with members of an Afrikaner rights group this year. Mr. Dunham did not attend the meeting.
match_headline: The Road to Trump’s Embrace of White South Africans

text: An article on Thursday about the novelist Françoise Sagan misidentified the poet from whom Ms. Sagan took the title of her debut novel, “Bonjour tristesse.” It is the second line of a poem by Paul Éluard, not Paul Valéry.
match_headline: The Literary ‘It’ Girl Who Continues to Fascinate

text: An article this weekend on Page 22 about the actress Meghann Fahy misidentified the name of the fruit bouquet featured in the Netflix series “Sirens.” It is the Delicious Party, not the Party Dipped Fruit Delight.
match_headline: In ‘Sirens,’ Meghann Fahy Sounds the Alarm

text: An article this weekend on Page 86 about Coco Chanel’s holiday home on the French Riviera misattributes the quote, “You dress or not as you choose for dinner.” The line was written by the Vogue correspondent Bettina Wilson about Chanel’s dinner parties at La Pausa; it was not said by Chanel.
ref_headline: You dress or not as you choose for dinner.
match_headline: Coco Chanel’s French Riviera Home Comes Back to Life

text: An article this weekend on Page 94 about the trio of artists known as PaJaMa refers incorrectly to the book “Paul Cadmus: 49 Drawings.” It is a portfolio of drawings by Cadmus, not the catalog of an exhibition at Hauser & Wirth last year.
match_headline: The Trio Whose Erotic Photographs Inspired a Generation of Artists

text: Because of an editing error, an obituary on Friday about the actor Joe Don Baker misstated the year the movie “Junior Bonner” was released. As noted correctly in a picture caption, it was 1972, not 1971.
match_headline: Joe Don Baker, Actor Who Found Fame With ‘Walking Tall,’ Dies at 89

text: An article on Friday about Poland’s upcoming presidential election misspelled the surname of Poland’s foreign minister. He is Radoslaw Sikorski, not Sikorksi. The article also misspelled the given name of a presidential candidate. She is Magdalena Biejat, not Magdelena.
match_headline: Is There Political Life After Populism? Poland May Be the Test.

text: An article on Monday about the legal industry website Above the Law misstated David Lat’s relationship with Above the Law. Mr. Lat was a founding editor of the website and retained a small stake in Breaking Media after he left the company in 2019; he is not the founder of the website, which was created and is owned by Breaking Media.
match_headline: The Website Where Lawyers Mock ‘Yellow-Bellied’ Firms Bowing to Trump

text: An obituary on Monday about Peter Lax, a pre-eminent Cold War mathematician, misspelled the first name of a mathematician who tutored Mr. Lax in his youth in Hungary. She was Rózsa Péter, not Rósza.
match_headline: Peter Lax, Pre-eminent Cold War Mathematician, Dies at 99

text: An article on Sunday about the Democrats’ next primary battle referred imprecisely to Mark Cuban’s political affiliation. While he has supported Democratic candidates, he has not publicly disclosed any party registration.
match_headline: Is Every Top Democrat Running for President?

text: An article on Tuesday about Richard Strauss’s opera “Salome” misstated the date of the Metropolitan Opera broadcast to theaters. It was planned for May 17, not May 24.
match_headline: Seven Takes on Opera’s Most Lurid Dance

text: An article on Sunday about the Swiss watchmaker Jaeger-LeCoultre referred incorrectly to the watch manufacturer. The brand is known for crafting the world’s smallest mechanical movement, not the world’s thinnest.
match_headline: An Amsterdam Hotel With Canal Views

text: An obituary on Sunday about the reproductive scientist Malcolm Potts misstated the number of women and girls who were raped by Pakistani soldiers during the Indo-Pakistan war of 1971. Estimates range from 200,000 to 400,000; the total was not 1,500, which was the number of women and girls whose pregnancies were terminated by Dr. Potts and his medical team after they were invited by the government to perform abortions on rape victims.
match_headline: Malcolm Potts, Irreverent Evangelist for Contraception, Dies at 90

text: A subhead with an article on Monday about a deal between New Jersey Transit and the Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and Trainmen reaching to end a three-day strike, misstated the number of passengers who use the commuter railroad each day. About 350,000 commuters use the system’s buses and trains daily, not just its trains.
match_headline: New Jersey Transit Engineers Strike, Idling Trains and Upending Commutes

text: An article on Thursday about the sentencing of three men who were convicted of murder, robbery and conspiracy for targeting patrons at night spots in Manhattan misquoted Judge Felicia A. Mennin. She said the victims’ lives had been less important to the defendants than “some fancy shoes and some fancy booze,” not “some fancy boots.”
ref_headline: some fancy shoes and some fancy booze,
match_headline: Three Sentenced for Luring Gay-Bar Robbery Victims to Their Deaths

text: An article on Tuesday about some of the life-altering advances in science that government investment made possible misstated the name of a creator of CAPTCHA technology. It is Manuel Blum, not Michael Blum.
match_headline: 9 Federally Funded Scientific Breakthroughs That Changed Everything

text: An article on Page 8 this weekend about the FX sitcom “Adults” misstates the show’s premiere date. It is Wednesday, May 28, not Thursday, May 29.
match_headline: For the Creators of ‘Adults,’ Maturity Is Overrated

text: A picture caption with an article on Saturday about Lebanon’s plan to disarm Palestinian factions in refugee camps on its territory reversed the identifications of Mahmoud Abbas, the Palestinian Authority president, and Nawaf Salam, the Lebanese prime minister. Mr. Abbas was on the left, and Mr. Salam on the right.
match_headline: Lebanon Moves to Disarm Palestinian Groups, a Test Run for Hezbollah

text: A picture caption with an article on Friday about the Senate’s vote to block California’s plan to phase out the sale of new gasoline-powered vehicles misstated the name of an observatory in Los Angeles. It is the Griffith Observatory, not Griffin.
match_headline: Senate Republicans Kill California’s Ban on Gas-Powered Cars

text: An article on Monday about the path Democrats are searching for after their losses in the 2024 election misstated the gender of a Democrat in Georgia who had spoken this year with Anat Shenker-Osorio, a Democratic researcher. The person was a man, not a woman.
match_headline: Six Months Later, Democrats Are Still Searching for the Path Forward

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Monday analyzing the off-season moves of the 12 teams in the 2024 College Football Playoff reversed the surnames of two former Penn State players. They are Tyler Warren and Abdul Carter, not Tyler Carter and Abdul Warren.
match_headline: Senator Tommy Tuberville of Alabama Will Run for Governor

text: A video game review on May 16 about Doom: The Dark Ages misidentified the composer of the soundtrack for the game. It was composed by the team known as Finishing Move, not Mick Gordon.
match_headline: A Frenzied Franchise Takes the Fight Up Close

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about new comedy specials available to stream misidentified the woman who told Sarah Silverman her hair was dry. It was her mother, not her stepmother.
match_headline: 6 Very Different Specials Worth Your Time on the Long Holiday Weekend

text: An article on Tuesday about the near completion of the Lucas Museum of Narrative Art in Los Angeles referred incorrectly to the museum’s acquisition of Judith Baca’s monumental mural “The History of California.” The museum acquired the archive for the mural, but not the mural itself.
match_headline: As George Lucas’s ‘Starship’ Museum Nears Landing, He Takes the Controls

text: An article on Tuesday about the methods that archaeologists are using to map some of the dozens of sunken vessels in Lake Ontario misidentified the prevailing flow of the St. Lawrence River. A ship would have sailed up the river en route to Oswego, not down.
match_headline: Historic Shipwrecks Come to Light in the Great Lakes

text: A picture caption with an article on Wednesday about President Trump’s interest in the gold reserves at Fort Knox misidentified the position that Ron Paul held in Congress. As the article correctly stated, he was a member of the U.S. House of Representatives, not the Senate. (His son Rand Paul serves in the Senate.)
match_headline: Trump Questioned Whether U.S. Gold is Missing. His Allies Are Trying to Cash In.

text: An article on Thursday about a new statue of the former Soviet dictator Joseph Stalin in a Moscow metro station and how it reflects Russia’s efforts to rehabilitate his memory misidentified the Russian court that shut down a human rights group, Memorial, in 2021. It was Russia’s Supreme Court, not the Moscow City Court.
match_headline: Stalin’s Image Returns to Moscow’s Subway, Honoring a Brutal History

text: An article on Sunday about the guest list to President Trump’s private dinner for customers of his cryptocurrency business misidentified one of the guests at the dinner. Joel Li, the chief executive of EV.com, attended, not Joel Lee, a software engineer from Singapore.
match_headline: Who Won a Seat at Trump’s Crypto Dinner?

text: An obituary on Wednesday about Ronnie Dugger, the founding editor of The Texas Observer, misstated the year he graduated from Brackenridge High School in San Antonio. It was 1947, not 1946.
match_headline: Ronnie Dugger, Crusading Texas Journalist, Dies at 95

text: An article on Friday about a landslide in the Swiss Alps on Wednesday misstated the distance between Blatten and Brienz, where a landslide narrowly missed in 2023, in Switzerland. They are about 140 miles apart, not 60. Another village of the same name, Brienz, is about 60 miles from Blatten.
match_headline: Glacier Collapse in the Swiss Alps Destroys Most of a Village

text: An article on May 18 about Rubik’s Cube misspelled the surname of an actor. He is Jim Carrey, not Carey.
match_headline: How the Rubik’s Cube Taught Me to Be a Better Parent

text: A picture caption with an article on Friday about concerns that organized crime could exert its influence in Mexico’s judicial elections misidentified the building shown near a street protest in Mexico City. It is the Palacio de Bellas Artes, not the Senate building.
match_headline: What to Know About Mexico’s Sweeping Judicial Election

text: An article on May 25 about an unconventional program that claims to reverse Alzheimer’s symptoms referred imprecisely to the sales of Dale Bredesen’s book “The End of Alzheimer’s.” The title has sold around 300,000 copies in the United States, not overall.
match_headline: An Expensive Alzheimer’s Lifestyle Plan Offers False Hope, Experts Say

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about a neighborhood on the outskirts of Berlin that was built for the elite guard of the Nazi Reich misidentified the spokesman for Vonovia, a company that serves as a landlord for some 300 apartments in the development was misidentified. He is Matthias Wulff, not Wulaaff.
match_headline: ‘Are the Bricks Evil?’ In a Village Built for Nazis, Darkness Lingers.

text: An article on Sunday about the Energy Department’s announcement that it was terminating $3.7 billion in Biden-era awards to companies trying to demonstrate technologies that might one day help tackle global warming misidentified the location of a Heidelberg Materials cement plant. It is in Indiana, not Louisiana.
match_headline: Energy Dept. Cancels $3.7 Billion for New Technologies to Lower Emissions

text: An article on Saturday about a federal grand jury indicting a former New Hampshire businessman, Eric Spofford, on charges that he orchestrated attacks on the homes of journalists who had investigated claims of sexual misconduct against him misstated how much money Mr. Spofford is accused of paying an associate to vandalize the homes of a reporter and an editor at New Hampshire Public Radio as well as the home of the reporter’s parents. It was $20,000, not $10,000.
match_headline: Businessman Accused of Orchestrating Attacks on Journalists

text: An article on Sunday about the actress and singer Megan Hilty’s routine on show days while she’s starring in the Broadway musical “Death Becomes Her” misstated Brian Gallagher’s age. He is 45, not 52.
match_headline: How Megan Hilty, a Tony Nominee, Spends Her Show Days

text: An obituary on May 23 about Jim Irsay, the owner and chief executive of the Indianapolis Colts of the National Football League, misstated the location of his death. It occurred in a hotel in Beverly Hills, Calif., not Los Angeles.
match_headline: Jim Irsay, Longtime Owner of the Indianapolis Colts, Dies at 65

text: An obituary on Thursday about Bruce Logan, a special effects artist and cinematographer, using information from David Zucker, one of the directors of “Airplane!,” misstated Mr. Logan’s contribution to that film. He worked on miniatures; he did not design the title sequence.
match_headline: Bruce Logan, Who Blew Up the Death Star in ‘Star Wars,’ Dies at 78

text: An article on Sunday about the discovery of the remains of a Canadian university student near a trail in the Adirondack Mountains trail described incorrectly the profession of Chayi Beaulieu. She is a behavior technician, not a social worker.
match_headline: The Hiker Who Couldn’t Be Found

text: An article on Wednesday about the large-scale deadly shooting near a food distribution center in southern Gaza on Tuesday referred imprecisely to the locations of the Gaza Humanitarian Foundation’s aid distribution sites in Gaza. They are mostly in the south of the enclave, and one is in central Gaza.
match_headline: Israeli Soldiers Open Fire Near Gaza Aid Site. Gaza Officials Say 27 Are Killed.

text: An article on Wednesday about the spread of 3D-printed firearms in the Aland Islands, an autonomous stretch of rocky coves in the middle of the Baltic Sea, misspelled the surname of an Aland Islands gunsmith and misstated the year he died. His name is Janne Stenroos, not Stenros, and he died in 2022, not 2023.
match_headline: How U.S. Gun Culture Spread to a Remote Island in the Baltic Sea

text: An obituary on Sunday about the actress Loretta Swit referred incorrectly to the film “Race With a Devil,” in which Ms. Swit appeared in 1975. It had a theatrical release; it was not a television movie. The obituary also misstated the rank of the father of Ms. Swit’s character, Major Houlihan, in the television series “M*A*S*H.” He was a colonel, not a general.
match_headline: Loretta Swit a.k.a. Hot Lips of TV’s ‘M*A*S*H,’ Dies at 87

text: An obituary on Sunday about George E. Smith, who shared a Nobel Prize in Physics for his discovery of a revolutionary imaging device, referred incorrectly to an interview he gave in 2001. It was for an oral history project conducted by the IEEE History Center in Piscataway, N.J., not with the Engineering and Technology History Wiki website. The obituary also misstated the number of pages in his noted doctoral dissertation at the University of Chicago in 1959. It was eight pages, not three. (At the time, it was the shortest Ph.D. dissertation in the university’s history.)
match_headline: George E. Smith, Nobel Winner Who Created a Digital Eye, Dies at 95

text: Because of an editing error, an obituary on Thursday about the writer Edmund White, who explored gay life in novels and memoirs, misspelled the original surname of his mother. She was born Delilah Teddlie, not Teddie.
match_headline: Edmund White, Pioneer of Gay Literature, Is Dead at 85

text: A picture caption with an article on Thursday about the United States vetoing a U.N. Security Council resolution demanding an immediate and unconditional cease-fire in Gaza, the release of all hostages and the resumption of full-scale humanitarian aid deliveries to the enclave misstated the position of Samuel Zbogar, Slovenia’s ambassador to the United Nations. He is not the president of the Security Council, though he formerly held that rotating position. The current president is Carolyn Rodrigues-Birkett, Guyana’s U.N. ambassador.
match_headline: U.S. Vetoes U.N. Resolution Demanding Immediate Gaza Cease-Fire

text: An article on Thursday about a Johnson & Johnson clinical trial for patients with multiple myeloma referred imprecisely to the company Legend Biotech. While it was based in China when it developed and released data on its CAR-T immunotherapy, the company is now a U.S. firm headquartered in New Jersey.
match_headline: From No Hope to a Potential Cure for a Deadly Blood Cancer

text: An article on Thursday about the Tony-nominated play “Purpose” misstated the year in which the show was staged at Chicago’s Steppenwolf Theater. It was in 2024, not 2023.
match_headline: For ‘Purpose’ Cast, Navigating Revisions Became a ‘Juggling Act’

text: An article on Thursday about the film festival Bleak Week: Cinema of Despair, described incorrectly the board of the American Cinematheque. It does not include Ted Sarandos; he left the board in 2021.
match_headline: Misery Loves Company? Bleak Week: Cinema of Despair Hits a Nerve.

text: An obituary on Wednesday about David Cope, a pioneer of algorithmic music compositions, misstated the year he joined the University of California, Santa Cruz. He was hired in 1977, not 1997.
match_headline: David Cope, Godfather of A.I. Music, Is Dead at 83

text: An article on Sunday about Hong Kong’s strategy to attract tourism misstated the timing of the Golden Week holiday. It is in early May, not early March.
match_headline: Hong Kong Looks for Ways to Win Back Big-Spending Tourists

text: An article on Monday about Representative Don Bacon, a Nebraska Republican, and his break with President Trump misstated the number of terms an incumbent mayor of Omaha had served before his recent election loss. It was three terms, not four.
match_headline: Breaking With Trump, Bacon Says He Won’t Follow His Party ‘Off the Cliff’

text: A picture caption with an article on Friday about a new show by the photographer Elle Pérez misstated the title of an artwork, using information from the Cultural Counsel. It is “Untitled (wet and tired flowers),” from “La Despedida,” 2025, not “Untitled (cut path and yellow flowers),” from “La Despedida,” 2025.
match_headline: Punk, Monet and Puerto Rico: New Photography From Elle Pérez

text: An article on Monday about the television producer Jesse Collins misidentified the documentary in which Beyoncé requests a 30-foot camera track. It is “Renaissance: A Film by Beyoncé,” not “Homecoming.”
match_headline: Why Beyoncé and BET Keep Calling Jesse Collins

text: An article on Thursday about the shared interests of labor unions and immigrants in the Los Angeles protests misstated the type of paperwork that the federal government flags when there is a mismatch with Social Security records. It is tax paperwork, not immigration verification paperwork.
match_headline: How Labor Unions Came to Define the L.A. Immigration Protests

text: An article on Thursday about a House-passed domestic policy bill incorrectly described the accounting of profit margins. They would appear on a company’s income statement, not its balance sheet.
match_headline: The House’s Policy Bill Would Lose Money. Could Trump’s Tariffs Replace It?

text: An article on Tuesday about addressing suicide deaths in Suriname by restricting access to the pesticide paraquat misstated its impact on the country’s suicide rate. It cut the pesticide suicide mortality in half, not the overall suicide rate.
match_headline: A Killer Within Easy Reach

text: An article on June 8 about academic freedom misstated an aspect of allegations made about Professor Amy Wax in a legal filing by the University of Pennsylvania in a lawsuit and omitted Wax’s response. The legal filing noted that Wax has been accused of suggesting to a student––not saying––that Black people are inferior to white people. The article should have noted that Wax denies the allegations.
match_headline: A Professor Was Fired for Her Politics. Is That the Future of Academia?

text: An article on Nov. 10 about Uyghur refugees described inaccurately an internal Chinese government spreadsheet. The spreadsheet contained the names of more than 16,000 people, not 17,743 Uyghurs. (Close to 1,000 entries on the spreadsheet appear to be duplicates. And some entries appear to represent Kazakhs, not Uyghurs.)
match_headline: He Made a Daring Escape From China. Then His Real Troubles Began.

text: An article this weekend on Page 12 about six hidden shops in New York misstates the profession of Daniel Corral-Webb, a co-founder of Archivio Records. He is a D.J., not a designer. The article also misstates the online presence of High Valley Books. The shop does have a website.
match_headline: ‘By Appointment Only’ in New York: 6 Hidden Shops Worth Visiting

text: An article on Saturday about the attacks in Israel and Iran on Friday misstated which Iranian official addressed the United Nations Security Council on Friday. It was Iran’s U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, not Iran’s foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi.
match_headline: Iran Retaliates and Israel Vows More Strikes After Devastating Attack

text: An article on Saturday about the victims of the Air India flight from Ahmedabad to London’s Gatwick Airport that crashed into the facilities of a nearby medical college misstated the surname of a family friend of one of the crash victims. He is Sanjay Jhajjar, not Chhibber.
match_headline: Lives Lost in India Crash: Expectant Grandparents, a Boy Selling Tea

text: An article on Saturday about the Trump administration’s shifting rationale for detaining Mahmoud Khalil after a federal judge ruled that the government could not continue to hold the Columbia University graduate under its initial justification misspelled the surname of a former homeland security official. He is Scott Shuchart, not Schuchart.
match_headline: Blocked by Judge, U.S. Shifts Rationale for Detaining Mahmoud Khalil

text: An article on Monday about the wedding of Huma Abedin and Alex Soros’s wedding in the Hamptons misstated Ms. Abedin’s age. She is 48, not 49.
match_headline: The Clintons and Kamala Harris Descend on a Hamptons Wedding of Liberal Royalty

text: An article on Friday about the six-episode Australian murder mini-series “The Survivors” misidentified the character Mia in the series. Mia is Kieran’s girlfriend and the mother of his child; she is not his wife.
match_headline: ‘The Survivors’ Is a Polished and Potent Murder Mystery

text: An obituary on June 11 about Frederick Forsyth, the author of “The Day of the Jackal” and other thrillers, referred incorrectly to an incident in 1973 when Mr. Forsyth, working for British intelligence, traveled to Dresden, which was then in East Germany. Although he recalled that Vladimir V. Putin was the K.G.B. station chief there at the time, Mr. Putin did not join the K.G.B. until 1975 and was not stationed in Dresden until 1985.
match_headline: Frederick Forsyth, Master of the Geopolitical Thriller, Dies at 86

text: An obituary on Sunday about Harris Yulin, the award-winning actor and director, misstated part of the name of a play in which Mr. Yulin appeared in 1963. It is “Next Time I’ll Sing to You,” not “Next Time I’ll Sing for You.”
ref_headline: Next Time I’ll Sing to You,
match_headline: Harris Yulin, Actor Who Perpetually Played the Bad Guy, Dies at 87

text: A video game review on Saturday about the role-playing adventure game Deltarune misidentified one of the character’s childhood friends. She is Noelle Holiday, not Carol.
match_headline: Lovable Misfits Make an Unfinished Game So Satisfying

text: A picture caption with an article on Thursday about President Trump’s shifted stance toward Iran under pressure from Israel misidentified which replicated Jerusalem building people in Iran were gathered around. The building is modeled after the Dome of the Rock, not Al Aqsa Mosque.
match_headline: How Trump Shifted on Iran Under Pressure From Israel

text: A picture caption with an article on Friday about an American artist who left the United States for Kyiv and was one of at least 28 people killed in a large-scale Russian drone and missile attack misspelled the surname of the individual. He was Fred Grandy, not Grady.
match_headline: This Oregon Native Went to Kyiv as a Volunteer. He Died in a Russian Attack.

text: An article on June 18 about Maj. Erica Vandal’s dismissal from the military because of President Trump’s transgender ban misstated the type of gun salute her father, Lt. Gen. Thomas Vandal, received at his funeral. It was a 15-gun salute, not a 21-gun salute.
match_headline: The Army Was the Only Life She Knew. Trump’s Trans Ban Cast Her Out.

text: An article on Saturday about Mahmoud Khalil, a pro-Palestinian campus protester detained by the Trump administration, being released on bail Friday misspelled the given name of a lawyer arguing on behalf of the government. He is Dhruman Sampat, not Drhuman Sampat.
match_headline: ‘Justice Will Prevail,’ Mahmoud Khalil Says as He Goes Free on Bail

text: An article on Sunday about New York City’s mayoral primary misstated the duration of early voting in the 2021 mayoral primary. It ran for nine days, not 10.
match_headline: N.Y.C. Mayoral Primary May Hinge on Early Voters as Heat Wave Looms

text: An article on June 1 about the Trump administration terminating a $258 million program to work on H.I.V. vaccines misspelled the name of a county in North Carolina. It is Mecklenburg, not Mecklenberg.
match_headline: Trump Administration Ends Program Critical to Search for an H.I.V. Vaccine

text: An article on Friday about some of the most noteworthy legislation that passed in June by New York State’s Legislature misspelled the surname of the state senator who cosponsored a bill meant to pre-empt any potential rollback of housing discrimination laws. He is Brian Kavanagh, not Kavanaugh.
match_headline: N.Y. Lawmakers Target Prison Reform and A.I. in Frantic End of Session

text: An article on Saturday about Adrienne Adams rejecting pressure to cross-endorse Zohran Mamdani for New York City mayor misstated the month that Mr. Mamdani made his first campaign appearance at a Black church. His first such visit was in February, not April.
match_headline: Mamdani Helped a Rival Candidate. She Won’t Return the Favor.

text: An article on Sunday about an experimental treatment that involved a single infusion of a stem cell-based treatment, which may have cured 10 out of 12 people with the most severe form of type 1 diabetes, misstated where new islet cells that were infused took up residence. It was in the liver, not the pancreas
match_headline: People With Severe Diabetes Are Cured in Small Trial of New Drug

text: An article on Monday about Mahmoud Khalil giving his first interview since his release from detention on Friday misspelled the given name of the author of “Man’s Search for Meaning.” He was Viktor Frankl, not Victor.
match_headline: ‘It Felt Like Kidnapping,’ Khalil Says in First Interview Since Release

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on June 18 about the claim of Herkimer, N.Y., that it, and not Springfield, Mass., is the actual birthplace of basketball misstated the given name of the game’s acknowledged inventor. He was Dr. James Naismith, not John.
match_headline: Who Really Invented Basketball? The ‘Human Calculator’ Thinks He Knows.

text: An article on June 13 about 30 television shows to watch this summer misstated the premiere date of the Apple TV+ series “Stillwater.” It is Aug. 1, not Aug. 4.
match_headline: 30 Shows to Watch This Summer

text: An article on June 19 about the challenges facing Art Basel’s flagship event in Switzerland misstated the planned size of Igor Lah’s private museum in Slovenia. It will be more than 50,000 square feet, not 25,000.
match_headline: Is Contemporary Art’s Most Important Fair Losing Its Luster?

text: An article on Tuesday, using information provided by a source about some of the discoveries that the Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile is expected to make, misstated how close an asteroid seen by Rubin will pass by Earth. It is 70,000 miles, not 60,000.
match_headline: This Powerful Telescope Quickly Found 2,100 New Asteroids

text: An article on Wednesday about South Korea’s concerns that China could use its large steel cages and a former oil drilling rig in the Yellow Sea to expand its military influence misspelled the name of one of the steel cages. It is the Shen Lan 2, not the Shan Lan 2.
match_headline: ​China Calls Them Fish Farms. South Korea Fears They Have Another Use.

text: An article on Thursday about Robert F. Kennedy’s decision to withdraw U.S. financial support from the leading international vaccine organization, Gavi, misstated how often a summit is held to replenish its finances. The summit is held every five years, not every four years.
match_headline: Kennedy Withdraws U.S. Funding Pledge to International Vaccine Agency

text: Because of an editing error, an article on June 21 about a French lawmaker’s efforts to reverse a decision to deny him entry to the United States referred imprecisely to the Electronic System for Travel Authorization program. It applies to international visitors to the United States from countries with certain visa waiver arrangements, not to all international visitors.
match_headline: French Lawmaker Says He Was Denied Entry Into the United States

text: An obituary on Tuesday about the architect Nathan Silver, whose 1967 book, “Lost New York,” discussed buildings that had been demolished before the city passed a landmarks preservation law, referred incorrectly to the Singer Tower in Manhattan. It was not included in that edition; although it was later demolished and was included in the 2000 edition, it was still standing at the time.
match_headline: Nathan Silver, Who Chronicled a Vanished New York, Dies at 89

text: An article on Friday about the impact that Republican efforts to reduce federal funding flowing to schools has had on Johns Hopkins University misstated the source of a Trump administration plan to cut about 40 percent of the budget for the National Institutes of Health. It is contained in a budget proposal, not in the Trump policy bill under consideration in Congress.
match_headline: Johns Hopkins Gets the Most Federal Money, but Now Much of It Is at Risk

text: An article on Thursday about U.S. rivals celebrating the Trump administration’s decision to cut its support for Voice of America and Radio Free stations referred incompletely to the number of followers on Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty’s Instagram accounts. It has 44,500 followers on its English-language account and a total of 17.4 million followers across the network’s various language services. It does not have a total of 44,500 followers.
match_headline: As U.S. Dismantles Voice of America, Rival Powers Hope to Fill the Void

text: An article this weekend on Page 12 about the Brazilian artist Luana Vitra includes outdated information. Hélio Menezes is no longer the director of the Museu Afro Brasil in São Paulo.
match_headline: The Brazilian Artist Who Listens to Minerals

text: An article on Saturday about Zohran Mamdani’s campaign promises while running for mayor of New York City referred incorrectly to the New York City Council. It is not the case that the Council has never passed a minimum wage law. It did so in 1964, but the law was struck down in court.
match_headline: Free Buses and Billions in New Taxes. Can Mamdani Achieve His Plans?

text: An article on Saturday about Susan Namangale and her efforts to introduce the game of chess in Malawi, misstated the size of the family into which Ms. Namangale was born. It was a family of eight, not seven.
match_headline: Chess Lover Introduces Game to Malawi’s Prisons, Schools and Street Kids

text: An article on Sunday about the four police officers in France who were ordered to stand trial on charges that they assaulted a Black man in November 2020 misspelled the name of the fourth police officer identified as a defendant by prosecutors. The officer identified by prosecutors is Hugues R., not Hughes R.
match_headline: French Police Officers Who Beat a Black Man on Camera to Stand Trial

text: An article on Sunday about the rise in political violence in the United States misstated the events that followed Thomas Crooks’s assassination attempt on Donald Trump at a Butler, Pa., campaign event. Mr. Crooks was not charged; he was killed by the Secret Service that day.
match_headline: Political Violence Is Part of the American Story. It Is Also Changing.

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about Republican Senator Thom Tillis’s announcement that he will not seek re-election in North Carolina next year misstated the political affiliation of Andy Nilsson, a candidate for the U.S. Senate from North Carolina. Mr. Nilsson is a Republican, not a Democrat.
match_headline: Tillis Announces He Won’t Run Again as Trump Threatens Him With a Primary

text: An article on Tuesday about a heat wave sweeping much of Europe incorrectly described Puerta del Sol. It is one of the largest squares in Madrid, not the largest.
match_headline: Europe’s Heat Wave Is Dangerous and Still Getting Worse

text: An article on Saturday about the Supreme Court’s decision to uphold a provision of the Affordable Care Act that requires insurance companies to offer some kinds of preventive care for free misstated the requirement of the Affordable Care Act’s individual mandate. The mandate requires that people obtain health insurance or pay a fine, not that most employers provide health insurance for their workers.
match_headline: Justices Uphold Preventive Care Provision in Affordable Care Act

text: An obituary on Tuesday about the former congressman Pat Williams misstated the number of grandchildren who survive him. It is three, not five.
match_headline: Pat Williams, 87, Last Montana Democrat to Serve in the House, Dies

text: An article on Wednesday about the Senate’s vote-a-rama on President Trump’s domestic policy bill, using information from Senate officials, referred incorrectly to the vote-a-rama session. It came close to breaking a record for the most recorded votes cast during such a session; it did not break that record.
match_headline: Senators Work All Night to Pass Trump’s Bill, Twisting Arms and Nearing a Record

text: An article on Wednesday about the sentencing of a man who killed a paramedic in a random attack in 2022 referred incorrectly to Alison Russo at the time she was attacked. She was walking near her station in Astoria, Queens. She was not walking back to it.
match_headline: Man Who Killed Paramedic in Random Attack Receives 25 Years to Life

text: An article on Wednesday about the singer and songwriter Neil Sedaka misstated the release date of his last major compositional effort, “Manhattan Intermezzo.” It was 2012, not 2016.
match_headline: Neil Sedaka Executed One of Pop’s Great Comebacks. Now, He Just Plays.

text: A headline with an article on Wednesday about an explosion at a pharmaceutical factory in the southern Indian state of Telangana misstated the number of people killed by the blast. As the article correctly noted it was 36, not 26.
match_headline: Explosion at Indian Pharmaceutical Factory Kills 36

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Thursday about a federal judge’s ruling that the Trump administration cannot categorically deny asylum claims from people crossing the southern border referred incorrectly to immigrant processing in Tapachula, Mexico. The city is in the south of Mexico, not near a U.S. port of entry.
match_headline: Judge Rejects Trump’s Attempt to Stop Asylum Claims at Border

text: An article on Page 38 this weekend about Luke Littler, widely considered to be the world’s best darts player, misstates the duration of the Premier League darts competition. It is four months long, not six.
match_headline: An 18-Year-Old Prodigy May Be Darts’ First Global Superstar

text: An article on Wednesday about four friends who took a weekend trip to Texas’s Hill Country before the catastrophic flooding began misstated Aidan Heartfield’s age. Mr. Heartfield, who remains missing, is 22, not 21.
match_headline: Two Found, Two Missing: An Agonizing Search After the Texas Floods

text: An article on Wednesday about notable new songs of the week misstated the release month for Trio of Bloom’s upcoming album. It is September, not November.
match_headline: Kesha Seeks a Chaotic Love, and 9 More New Songs

text: An obituary on Sunday about the entrepreneur S. Daniel Abraham misstated when his marriage to Estanne Weiner ended in divorce. It was 1985, not 1993.
match_headline: S. Daniel Abraham, Who Reaped Riches With Slim-Fast, Dies at 100

text: An article on Wednesday about the Oloffson Hotel in Haiti being burned to the ground last Saturday misstated the nationality of the family that took over the hotel. The family, the Oloffsons, were German-Haitian, not Norwegian-Haitian.
match_headline: A Hotel Made Famous by Graham Greene Is a Victim of Haiti’s Violence

text: An article on July 5 about the ways that Yemeni cafes are building momentum and community misspelled the first name of a commercial real estate investor. He is Don Tepman, not Dan.
match_headline: Why Do Yemeni Coffeehouses Seem to Be Everywhere Lately?

text: An article this weekend on Page 6 about New Jersey Transit’s locomotive 4207, the oldest in regular passenger service in the entire country, misstated the name of the NJ Transit maintenance site. It is the Meadows Maintenance Complex, not the Meadowlands Maintenance Complex.
match_headline: Locomotive 4207, on the Rails for 60 Years and Still Hauling Commuters

text: An article on Monday about how deep-sea cables that power the world are made misstated how much it cost to build a vessel, the Monna Lisa. It was about 250 million euros, not 30 million. In addition, the article referred incorrectly to the insulation that encases wires in high-capacity cables. The insulation is made from polyethylene, not polypropylene.
match_headline: How the Deep Sea Cables That Power the World Are Made

text: An article on Monday about what to watch on television this week described incorrectly the history of the show “Transplant.” It has been airing on NBC for all seasons; it is not being shown in the United States for the first time.
match_headline: ‘The Summer I Turned Pretty,’ Plus 6 Things to Watch on TV This Week

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday about former Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo of New York deciding to run in the general election for New York City mayor referred imprecisely to the political leanings of the cable news network NewsNation. It covers various perspectives, not just conservative ones.
match_headline: Cuomo to Fight On in Mayor’s Race After Bruising Primary Loss to Mamdani

text: An article on Monday about a new case against Tesla stemming from a 2019 crash of a Tesla Model S sedan with the Autopilot engaged made an incorrect distinction about the trial in Miami. It is the first trial over Tesla’s Autopilot system to reach a federal jury, not the first trial over Autopilot ever to be heard by a jury.
match_headline: Tesla Faces First Federal Jury Trial Tied to Its Autopilot System

text: An article on Tuesday about the hair loss medication finasteride potentially leading to sperm count reductions misquoted Dr. Kian Asanad, the director of the USC Fertility and Men’s Sexual Health Center, and placed his comments out of context. He said, “I always think about that on the back of my mind as something contributing or playing a role,” and, elsewhere, “I don’t think it’s as common as we really think.” He did not say, “I keep it in the back of my mind as a possible factor, but I would say it’s a less common thing.” In addition, the article omitted funding information related to a 1999 study that found no effects from finasteride on sperm count. The researchers received support from the pharmaceutical company Merck & Co.
ref_headline: I always think about that on the back of my mind as something contributing or playing a role,
match_headline: He Wanted a Fuller Head of Hair. He Got a Lower Sperm Count.

text: A news analysis article on Wednesday about how many Republicans in Congress, following the lead of President Trump, are changing their stance on sending aid to Ukraine referred imprecisely to Representative Troy Nehls’s affiliation with the House Freedom Caucus. He was part of the group but is no longer a member.
match_headline: Republicans in Congress Shift to Backing Ukraine, Matching Trump’s Reversal

text: An article on Wednesday about how the Justice Department is accelerating its efforts to undo decades of civil service protections intended to insulate the work of law enforcement officials from political interference misstated the name of an independent agency that considers appeals from federal workers about employment actions. It is the Merit Systems Protection Board, not the Merit Service Protection Board.
match_headline: Dismissals at Justice Dept. Would Bypass Civil Service and Whistle-Blower Laws

text: An article on Thursday about the return of a looted mosaic to the city of Pompeii referred imprecisely to some of the volcanic matter Pompeii was buried in when Mount Vesuvius erupted. It was buried in ash and rock fragments, not lava.
match_headline: A Pompeii Mosaic, Taken by a Nazi Captain, Is Returned

text: A video game review on Thursday about Donkey Kong Bananza misidentified the actress voicing Pauline. She is Jenny Kidd, not Kate Higgins.
match_headline: There’s a Lot to Dig in Donkey Kong Bananza

text: An article on Wednesday about an excavation in Ireland where the remains of hundreds of children are thought to have been buried described incorrectly the location of some disposed bodies. It was an old sewage system, not septic tanks.
match_headline: Dig for Children’s Remains Begins at Irish Home for Unwed Mothers

text: An article on Thursday about the Chinese A.I. industry misquoted Jia Haojun, the founder of Deep Principle. He said it would be a huge benefit if the government helped cover “13 to 15 percent” of early-stage research costs, not “10 to 15 percent.”
match_headline: China Is Spending Billions to Become an A.I. Superpower

text: An article on Monday about the biotech company Sarepta Therapeutics defying the Food and Drug Administration’s request that it halt distribution of its treatment for a deadly muscle-wasting disease misstated the subset of Duchenne patients for whom the treatment was first approved. It was approved for those who do not use wheelchairs, not those who do.
match_headline: Drugmaker Refuses F.D.A. Request to Pull Treatment Linked to Patient Deaths

text: An article on Thursday about changes to the Williamstown Theater Festival, using information from the festival, misstated last year’s budget. It was $5.7 million, not $4.7 million.
match_headline: Williamstown Theater Festival Was in Crisis. Here’s How It’s Changing.

text: An article on Sunday about the actor and singer Mandy Patinkin and his family incorrectly misstated the number of Tony Awards that Mr. Patinkin has won. While he was nominated multiple times, he has won only one, for “Evita.”
match_headline: The Grody-Patinkin Family Is a Mess. People Love It.

text: An article on Sunday about the legal implications of being an organ donor misstated the number of Americans who are in the national donor registry. There are more than 173 million Americans in both state registries and the national registry, not the national registry alone.
match_headline: You Said ‘Yes’ to Being an Organ Donor. What Exactly Does That Mean?

text: An article on Tuesday about President Trump urging two professional sports teams to change their names misstated the number of carved figures, known as the Guardians of Traffic, on the Hope Memorial Bridge in Cleveland. There are eight, not two.
match_headline: With Posts on Sports Team Names, Trump Wades Into a Decades-Old Dispute

text: An obituary on Tuesday about the actor Kenneth Colley misstated the cause of the character Admiral Piett’s death in the movie “Return of the Jedi.” His ship crashes into the Death Star; he is not shot down by a Rebel fighter.
match_headline: Kenneth Colley, 87, ‘Star Wars’ Actor With a Commanding Presence, Dies

text: A map with an article on Friday about recent military drills by China’s aircraft carriers in the Pacific reversed the colors of two carriers in the map’s key. The Shandong was in Hong Kong, not the Liaoning.
match_headline: China’s Aircraft Carriers Push Into Waters Long Dominated by U.S.

text: An obituary on Wednesday about the singer Ozzy Osbourne misstated the location of an incident that led to his being banned from performing in San Antonio. It happened at the Alamo Cenotaph, a memorial just outside the walls of the Alamo — not at the Alamo itself. The obituary also misstated the original surname of Mr. Osbourne’s mother. It was Unitt, not Levy.
match_headline: Ozzy Osbourne, ‘Prince of Darkness’ Turned Reality TV Star, Dies at 76

text: Because of an editing error, an obituary on Monday about the Olympic runner and track coach Bill Dellinger misidentified one of the standout runners he coached. He is Matt Centrowitz, an Olympian who won four United States national championships in the 5,000-meter run from 1979 to 1982, not his son, Matthew Centrowitz Jr., who won the gold medal in the 1,500 meters at the 2016 Olympics in Rio de Janeiro.
match_headline: Bill Dellinger, Runner and Track Coach Who Mentored Stars, Dies at 91

text: An article on July 18 about a federal judge’s move to block a rule intended to make it easier for many Americans to get loans by removing medical debt from credit reports referred imprecisely to a part of Judge Sean D. Jordan’s decision. Judge Jordan wrote that state bans on reporting medical debts to credit agencies would be pre-empted by the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act, but he did not issue a ruling pre-empting those bans.
match_headline: Judge Scraps Rule Eliminating Medical Debt on Credit Reports

text: An article on Thursday about Canada’s measles outbreak misstated which officials were urging the Alberta provincial leader to declare a public health emergency. They were public health experts, not provincial officials.
match_headline: Canada’s Measles Outbreak Exceeds Cases in the U.S.

text: An article on Friday about the powerful earthquake that struck off the coast of Russia’s Kamchatka Peninsula early Wednesday misstated the energy difference between earthquake magnitudes. A magnitude 9 quake has about three times as much energy as magnitude 8.7, not 10 times as much. It has about twice as much energy as magnitude 8.8, not three times as much.
match_headline: Why Did Such a Powerful Earthquake Produce Such a Weak Tsunami?

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about the impact of President Trump’s shifting tariff levels on the African nation of Lesotho misstated the day Lesotho’s 15 percent tariff rate was announced. It was Thursday night, not Friday night.
match_headline: How the Threat of Trump’s Highest Tariff Derailed an African Nation

text: An article on Monday about a city in Kansas suing over a planned ICE detention center misstated the language in a poster seen at a protest of an immigration detention facility in Leavenworth, Kan. The poster said that Leavenworth is “more than a prison town,” rather than “not just a prison town.”
match_headline: This City of Prisons Is Suing Over a Planned ICE Detention Center

text: An article on Friday about Ford Motor announcing that it lost money in the second quarter as tariffs took a toll on its business misstated the day that Ford reported its second-quarter earnings. It was Wednesday, not Tuesday.
match_headline: Ford Is Latest Carmaker to Blame Tariffs for Profit Slump

text: A picture from the streaming outlet TBPN published with an article on Friday about A.I. researchers’ pay packages misidentified a Microsoft employee who used to work at Google’s DeepMind lab. The person shown in the image was not Amar Subramanya.
match_headline: A.I. Researchers Are Negotiating $250 Million Pay Packages. Just Like N.B.A. Stars.

text: An article on Saturday about the negative impact that the Trump administration’s tariffs are having on businesses they were meant to help misstated the month that the United States lost 11,000 manufacturing jobs. It was July, not June. The article also misstated the number of manufacturing job losses in June, based on initial estimates. The revised number was 15,000, not 6,000. The earlier estimate was 6,000.
match_headline: Harm or Help? Why Companies Are Battling Tariffs Meant to Benefit Them.

text: An article on Sunday about a veteran lifeguard’s Friday routine misstated, in some instances, Javier Rodriguez’s surname on second reference and that of his three adult children. Their surname is Rodriguez, not Hernandez.
match_headline: How a Veteran Lifeguard Spends His Fridays

text: An article on Sunday about the Vatican’s six-day celebration of youth in the church referred incorrectly to some of the festivities. They were part of the Jubilee of Youth, not World Youth Day.
match_headline: With Festivities and Papal Prayer, the Vatican Aims to Attract the Young

text: An article on Monday about what to watch on television this week misstated the premiere time of “American Prince: J.F.K. Jr.” on CNN. It will premiere at 9 p.m. Eastern on Saturday, Aug. 9, not 10 p.m.
match_headline: Las Culturistas Culture Awards, Plus 5 Things to Watch on TV This Week

text: An article on Tuesday about jurists losing faith in President Trump’s Justice Department misstated Andrew Wiederhorn’s role with the restaurant chain Fatburger. He was previously a chief executive of the chain and founded its parent company. He did not create the restaurant chain.
match_headline: Judges Openly Doubt Government as Justice Dept. Misleads and Dodges Orders

text: A picture caption with an article on Wednesday about a growing debate in Japan between those who defend pacifism as a national virtue and those who think the country must abandon it referred incorrectly to a treaty that Japan has not signed. It is the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons, not the Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty.
match_headline: Even Hiroshima’s Pacifist Cause Is Losing Believers

text: An article on Wednesday about how the Trump administration will gain access to the standardized test scores and grade point averages of all applicants to Columbia and Brown University misstated the findings of an SAT report from the College Board. The report found that 1 percent of the African American high school graduates who took the SAT in 2024 scored between 1400 and 1600, the highest possible scores, and 27 percent of the Asian graduates scored at that level. It is not the case that, of all the high school graduates who scored between 1400 and 1600 in 2024, 1 percent were African American and 27 percent were Asian.
match_headline: Columbia and Brown to Disclose Admissions and Race Data in Trump Deal

text: An article on July 31 about Brazil’s President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva voicing frustration with President Trump over tariffs misidentified birds roaming the presidential palace lawns. They were rheas, not emus.
match_headline: No One Is Defying Trump Like Brazil’s President

text: An article on Wednesday about how Texas Republicans are hoping the surge of Hispanic support for President Trump in 2024 will last through the 2026 midterm elections misstated the percentage of votes that Representative Henry Cuellar, Democrat of Texas, won in Starr County in the 2024 elections. It was 69 percent, not 60 percent.
match_headline: G.O.P. Bets Big on Latino Voters With New Texas Map

text: An article on Wednesday about the Trump administration’s plans to terminate $7 billion in federal grants intended to help low- and moderate-income families install solar panels on their homes misstated the amount of money the Georgia Bright Communities Coalition was using to provide free rooftop solar panels to about 800 Georgia households. It is about $12 million of a $156 million Solar for All grant, not the entire $156 million grant amount.
match_headline: E.P.A. Moves to Cancel $7 Billion in Grants for Solar Energy

text: An article on Monday about businesses rushing goods out of duty-free warehouses to bonded warehouses, allowing foreign companies a way to postpone the payment of tariffs until the goods are sent to stores or directly to customers, misidentified James Marley of Swap Commerce. He is the head of operations at Swap Commerce, not the chief executive.
match_headline: Ahead of Trade Deadline, Firms Rushed Goods Out of Duty-Free Warehouses

text: An article on Wednesday about ESPN’s new streaming service starting misstated the stakes of Disney and Hearst in ESPN. Disney will own 72 percent of ESPN, not 70 percent, and Hearst will own 18 percent, not 20 percent.
match_headline: ESPN’s Streaming Era Will Start on Aug. 21

text: An article on Tuesday about political historian Heather Cox Richardson preparing for the narrator role in Aaron Copland’s “Lincoln Portrait” misstated her surname. It is Richardson, not Cox Richardson.
match_headline: Heather Cox Richardson Enters the History of ‘Lincoln Portrait’

text: An article on Tuesday about Representative Nancy Mace announcing her run for governor of South Carolina referred incorrectly to President Trump’s primary victory in South Carolina. The state was his second primary victory in 2016, not his first.
match_headline: Nancy Mace Announces Run for Governor of South Carolina

text: An article on Thursday about the Royal Ballet and Opera canceling a collaboration with the Israeli Opera to stage “Tosca” in Tel Aviv next year misidentified the opera at which a dancer staged a protest during the curtain call. It was “Il Trovatore,” not “La Traviata.”
match_headline: British Opera Company Cancels ‘Tosca’ Collaboration in Israel After Criticism

text: A picture caption with an obituary on July 19 about the editorial cartoonist Steve Benson misstated the given name of a former governor of Arizona, whom he often lampooned. As the obituary correctly notes, he was Evan Mecham, not Alfred.
match_headline: Steve Benson, a Skewering, Pulitzer-Winning Cartoonist, Dies at 71

text: An obituary on July 28 about the songwriter Tom Lehrer misstated his mother’s given name. It was Anna, not Alma.
match_headline: Tom Lehrer, Musical Satirist With a Dark Streak, Dies at 97

text: An obituary on Saturday about James A. Lovell Jr., the commander of the three-man Apollo 13 spacecraft that survived a near-catastrophic explosion as it approached the moon in April 1970, misstated how much time elapsed before Apollo 13 detected signs of trouble. It was 56 hours, not 56 minutes. It also misidentified the astronaut who was to accompany him to the surface of the moon in a lunar module, though that mission was never accomplished. He was Fred W. Haise Jr., not John L. Swigert Jr. Mr. Swigert was to remain behind in the orbiting Apollo 13 spaceship. The obituary also misstated how many great-grandchildren Captain Lovell had. It is nine, not eight.
match_headline: James A. Lovell Jr., Commander of Apollo 13, Is Dead at 97
