text: An article and related map on Aug. 1 about the countries that recognize a Palestinian state, relying on a U.N. document from April 2024, misstated the Czech Republic’s position on the recognition of a Palestinian state. While Czechoslovakia recognized Palestinian statehood in 1988, the Czech Republic recognizes the goal of an independent Palestinian state, but does not currently recognize it as one.
match_headline: The Changing Map of Palestinian Recognition

text: An article on Thursday about Britain’s charities’ regulator clearing Prince Harry of bullying, harassment and sexism at the charity he co-founded misstated the year that Sophie Chandauka became a trustee of Sentebale. It was 2008, not 2009. Because of an editing error, the article also misstated the number of trustees left on the Sentebale board after the resignations on March 24. Five trustees resigned, leaving only Ms. Chandauka on the board, not five of nine. Ms. Chandauka appointed four new trustees on March 25.
match_headline: Prince Harry Cleared of Bullying Accusations at Charity He Co-founded

text: An article on Monday about the announcement that the Row NYC in Times Square will stop operating as a housing shelter for migrants misstated the ownership of the Row NYC. Rockport Group, a Boston-based real estate private equity firm, no longer has a stake in the hotel.
match_headline: New York City to Close Last Major Hotel Housing Migrants

text: An article on Monday about the weekend’s box office earnings misstated the rating of the “Naked Gun” remake. It is PG-13, not R.
match_headline: ‘Weapons’ Gives Warner Bros. Another Outstanding No. 1 Opening

text: An article on Saturday about the music label Penrose Records and its influence in a soft-touch style of soul music misspelled the surname of an artist on the label’s roster. She is Vicky Tafoya, not Trafoya.
match_headline: California’s ‘Souldies’ Scene Puts a Fresh Spin on a Beloved Sound

text: An article on Tuesday about Quebec City’s small English-speaking community misspelled the name of a photographer and filmmaker. She is Sandra Tomaylla, not Sandra Tomayla.
match_headline: A Haven for English in the Most French of North American Cities

text: An article on Aug. 3 about corporations and individuals, including many in the crypto business, that wrote big checks to MAGA Inc. while seeking favor from President Trump misstated the total amount of money that MAGA Inc. received from crypto interests during the first half of this year, as well as the combined amount donated by Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss and their crypto exchange, Gemini. The total amount that MAGA Inc. received from crypto interests was nearly $42 million, not nearly $45 million. The Winklevosses combined to donate more than $1 million, not nearly $4 million. Their crypto exchange, Gemini, processed donations to MAGA Inc. made by other donors; it did not donate its own funds.
match_headline: Donor List Suggests Scale of Trump’s Pay-for-Access Operation

text: An article on Friday about the ways that Republicans shaped the debate over Sydney Sweeney’s American Eagle ad campaign misstated the date of one of Libs of TikTok’s social media posts on the American Eagle ad. While attention did shift with earlier posts by the influential account on July 27, the “Keep this up Democrats” comment cited in the article was published on July 28, not July 27.
match_headline: How the Right Shaped the Debate Over the Sydney Sweeney Ads

text: An article on Wednesday about the shooting outside a Target store in Austin, Texas, on Monday, misstated, using information from an initial police report, the name of the Target employee who died in the attack. The name was Rosa Machuca, not Hector Leopoldo Martinez Machuca. (The article was updated after the police released an update on the employee’s name.)
match_headline: Victims of Austin Gunman Included Grandfather and 4-Year-Old Granddaughter

text: An article this weekend on Page 120 about censorship of creative works contains several errors. It refers incorrectly to the artist Dread Scott’s 1989 installation “What Is the Proper Way to Display a U.S. Flag?” The exhibit offered visitors the choice of stepping on a flag while answering that question in a guest book; it did not require them to step on the flag. The article misstates the number of photographs of soldiers’ coffins draped with American flags in the installation. There was one such image, not multiple images. The article also misstates the artist Andres Serrano’s number of solo exhibitions in his career. He’s had about 20 solo museum exhibitions in Europe and abroad, and many fewer in the United States — not about 20 solo in Europe and only one in the United States. And because of a production error, the article misquotes John Waters on what a Maryland film censor said to him about his 1974 feature “Female Trouble.” Waters said she told him, “You can’t have that cunnilingus scene,” not “You can have that cunnilingus scene.”
ref_headline: What Is the Proper Way to Display a U.S. Flag?
match_headline: What Happened When Their Art Was Banned

text: A picture caption with an article this weekend on Page 140 about the artist Robert Longo, relying on information from a publicist, misstates the materials used in one of his works. It is a charcoal drawing of a pit of snakes, not an ink-and-charcoal drawing.
match_headline: What Does It Mean to Be a ‘Very American’ Artist Now?

text: A news analysis article on Saturday about President Trump’s summit meeting with President Vladimir V. Putin and the way it marked a return to imperial thinking, citing an expert, misstated the time frame for three divisions of Poland in the 18th century. It was between 1772 and 1795, not 1792 and 1795.
match_headline: How the Trump-Putin Summit Signals a Return to Imperial Thinking

text: An article on Sunday about the indictment of LaToya Cantrell, the mayor of New Orleans, for her actions related to a romantic relationship she had with a city police officer assigned to protect her, misidentified the New Orleans television station that staked out a city-owned apartment in the French Quarter. It was WVUE-TV, not WWL-TV.
match_headline: Mayor of New Orleans Is Indicted on Corruption Charges

text: An article on Sunday about the scrutiny that Representative Rob Bresnahan Jr. of Pennsylvania is facing over his stock trades referred incorrectly to the day of Mr. Bresnahan’s wedding. It was last Saturday, not next Saturday.
match_headline: Congressman’s Stock Trades Draw More Scrutiny After Key Votes

text: An obituary on Sunday about Robin Lakoff, a linguist who analyzed the ways women speak, referred incorrectly to the connection between Dr. Lakoff’s writings and a best-selling book by Deborah Tannen, “You Just Don’t Understand: Women and Men in Conversation.” The book did not popularize some of Dr. Lakoff’s ideas; rather, its subject matter, language and gender were a field that Dr. Lakoff inspired.
ref_headline: You Just Don’t Understand: Women and Men in Conversation.
match_headline: Robin Lakoff, Expert on Language and Gender, Is Dead at 82

text: An obituary on Monday about the art collector and critic Doris Lockhart Saatchi misstated the number of years she was married to Charles Saatchi. It was 17, not 14.
match_headline: Doris Lockhart Saatchi, 88, Critic and Collector of Cutting-Edge Art, Dies

text: Because of a production error, an article on Sunday about why the U.S. government is offering a $5 million reward for information leading to the arrest or conviction of a high-profile Haitian gang leader was missing the full name and identification of a man arrested in Texas in July. He is Bazile Richardson, a Haitian American trucker in North Carolina.
match_headline: Why Is the U.S. Offering a $5 Million Reward for a Haitian Gang Leader?

text: An article on Tuesday about the International Energy Agency forecasts suggesting that global demand for oil and gas could peak by the end of the decade misspelled the surname of the European Union energy minister. He is Dan Jorgensen, not Jorgenson.
match_headline: Will Oil Demand Peak Soon? Trump Administration Doesn’t Want to Hear It.

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about potential red flags that have popped up this summer heading into the next N.B.A. season misstated the number of red flags. There are eight, not 10.
match_headline: Beauty Essentials to Help You Beat the Heat This Summer

text: An article on Saturday about factory-built houses that are the latest attempt by FEMA to address a longtime need to upgrade temporary emergency housing misspelled the given name of the director of operations of Liv-Connected. She is Allysa Taylor, not Allyssa.
match_headline: In Lahaina, ‘Dignified’ Havens for Wildfire Survivors

text: An article on Aug. 2 about Edinburgh’s Festival Fringe described incorrectly the ’70s band that features in “Philosophy of the World.” The band, the Shaggs, was a real band, not a fictional one.
match_headline: Edinburgh Fringe Can Bring Artists Fame, but Money’s Another Matter

text: An article on Thursday about Israel approving new settlements in the West Bank and announcing that it was moving ahead with plans to take over Gaza City misspelled the surname of an Israeli analyst and former military intelligence officer. He is Michael Milshtein, not Milstein.
match_headline: With Moves on West Bank and Gaza City, Israel Defies Global Outcry

text: An article on Wednesday about the African Union endorsing a campaign to have organizations around the world replace the Mercator map with other alternatives referred imprecisely to the nationality of Gerardus Mercator. He was from what is now Belgium, not Germany.
match_headline: Africa Is Big, and It Wants the World’s Maps to Show It

text: An obituary on Monday about the actor Terence Stamp misstated when he entered an ashram. It was in the middle or late 1970s, not 1969. The obituary also referred incorrectly to “Billy Budd,” by Herman Melville, which was the basis of a 1962 movie starring Mr. Stamp. It is a novella, not a novel.
match_headline: Terence Stamp, 87, Dies; British Cinema Luminary and ‘Superman’ Villain

text: An obituary on Wednesday about the political reporter and columnist Jules Witcover misidentified the New Jersey high school he attended. It was Union Hill High School — not Union City High School, which was created when two schools, Emerson and Union Hill, merged in 2008.
match_headline: Jules Witcover, Political Reporter and Columnist, Dies at 98

text: An article on Sunday about how Michael Boulos, husband of Tiffany Trump, benefited financially from marrying into the Trump family referred incorrectly to Mar-a-Lago. It is a club, not a golf club.
match_headline: He Asked Tiffany Trump to Marry Him. Then the Deals Started Coming.

text: An article on Monday about the Coast Guard’s removal of buoys along the Northeast misspelled the given name of the Pilgrim leader who nearly hit the Mary Ann Rocks in December 1620. He was Myles Standish, not Miles.
match_headline: New England Boaters Battle a Coast Guard Plan to Remove Beloved Buoys

text: An article on Tuesday about efforts to build parks along Lower Manhattan’s waterfront referred imprecisely to proposed alternatives to a senior housing development in the area. While the alternatives remain uncertain, it is not the case that no developer has expressed interest in them.

text: An article on Monday about the top 25 potential M.L.B. free agents for 2025-26 referred incorrectly to a pitcher’s league affiliation. Framber Valdez of the Houston Astros finished in the top 10 in Cy Young Award voting in each of the past three seasons in the American League, not the National League.
match_headline: Workers Who Lost Jobs to Trade Needed Help. Washington Cut the Funding.

text: An article about President Trump’s heading overseas to persuade other countries to choose fossil fuels over renewable energy sources described incorrectly the setting in which Chris Wright, the energy secretary, called net-zero targets a “sinister goal.” He made the remarks remotely, via video — not in person in London.
match_headline: Trump, With Tariffs and Threats, Tries to Strong-Arm Nations to Retreat on Climate Goals

text: An article about federal funding cuts affecting American history content on PBS referred incorrectly to the series “Independent Lens,” produced by ITVS. The show co-produces most of the documentaries it airs; it does not exclusively acquire already completed films.
match_headline: As PBS Stations Confront Cuts, American History Takes a Hit

text: A picture caption with an article on Friday about how few people remain in Paris during the month of August misidentified an island in the River Seine. It is Île de la Cité, not Île St.-Louis.
match_headline: August in Paris: When the City Empties Out, Locals Left Behind Rejoice

text: An article on Thursday about a film series at the Museum of Modern Art marking the 20th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina misstated the number of fatalities attributable to the disaster. It is 1,392, not 1,833. The article also misidentified the setting of the film “The Beasts of the Southern Wild.” The film takes place in a fictional setting inspired in part by New Orleans’s Lower Ninth Ward, not literally in the Ninth Ward.
ref_headline: The Beasts of the Southern Wild.

text: A review on Thursday for the film “The Roses” misidentified the California community to which the couple moves. It is Mendocino, not Montecito.
match_headline: ‘The Roses’ Review: To Honor, Cherish, Envy and Despise

text: A review on Friday for the film “The Thursday Murder Club” misidentified the agency that the character Elizabeth works for. It is MI6, not MI5.
match_headline: ‘Thursday Murder Club’ Review: A Whodunit With Helen Mirren

text: An article on Friday about what to see in New York City galleries misstated the name of the gallery where Jane Dickson’s work is on view. It is Karma, not Karma Karma.
match_headline: What to See in N.Y.C. Galleries in August

text: An article on page 12 this weekend about the de Young Museum in San Francisco reinstalling its Native art galleries misstated the reason 12 positions were eliminated at the Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco. The 12 were cut as a result of increased costs and declines in attendance, not because of a reduction in municipal funding.
match_headline: Museum’s Update Sends a Message: Native Artists Are Still Here

text: A picture caption with an article on page 20 this weekend about children of Syrian detainees who were placed in orphanages by the government of Bashar al-Assad misidentified the orphanage shown in the photograph. It is a SOS Children’s Villages care center in Qudssaya, not the Lahn al-Hayat orphanage.
match_headline: They Were Treated Like Orphans. But They Knew the Truth.

text: An article on Sunday about the Lore bathing club opening in Manhattan misstates the cost of a monthly pass. It is $200, not $179. (The price changed after the article went to press.)
Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about bed and mattress shopping inadvertently referred to cotton as an animal material rather than a plant.
match_headline: Is Bed Shopping Turning into a Consumer’s Nightmare?

text: An obituary on Aug. 15 about the actress Danielle Spencer misstated how many episodes of the television show “What’s Happening!!” she appeared in. It was 63, not 65.
match_headline: Danielle Spencer, Child Star of ‘What’s Happening!!,’ Is Dead at 60

text: An obituary on Aug. 16 about the lawyer Gerry Spence referred imprecisely to the subject of his book “The Martyrdom of Collins Catch the Bear.” Although Mr. Spence wrote that Mr. Catch the Bear was wrongfully charged with murder, he, in fact, pleaded guilty to second-degree manslaughter.
ref_headline: The Martyrdom of Collins Catch the Bear.

text: An obituary on Monday about the writer Joan Mellen referred incorrectly to the novelist Kay Boyle, whose biography she wrote. She was not Southern.
match_headline: Joan Mellen, Whose Bobby Knight Biography Sparked Debate, Dies at 83

text: An article on Tuesday about the military-grade weapons that Mexico’s most powerful criminal groups are amassing misstated the name of the leader of an anti-bomb unit in Michoacán. He is Capt. Carlos Gómez, not Mario.
match_headline: With Drones and I.E.D.s, Mexico’s Cartels Adopt Arms of Modern War

text: An article on Monday about the deadly floods in Pakistan’s eastern Punjab Province misstated the name of Pakistan’s disaster management body. It is the National Disaster Management Authority, not Agency.
match_headline: Deadly Floods in Punjab Devastate Pakistan’s Breadbasket

text: An article on Tuesday about the more than 445,000 federal employees who saw their union protections disappear in August misidentified the location of a veterans’ medical center employing Irma Westmoreland, a nurse and union official. The medical center is in Augusta, Ga., not Atlanta.
match_headline: Trump Orders Have Stripped Nearly Half a Million Federal Workers of Union Rights

text: An article on Wednesday about Belgium’s decision to recognize a Palestinian state at this month’s United Nations General Assembly misstated the month that President Emmanuel Macron of France announced that the country would recognize a Palestinian state. It was July, not August.
match_headline: Belgium to Recognize Palestinian State, Joining Pressure on Israel

text: A picture caption with an article on Aug. 29 about the news that The Atlanta Journal-Constitution will stop publishing a print newspaper at the end of the year misstated the year of an archived edition of the paper shown in the image. The edition was from 1954, not 1942.
match_headline: Atlanta Journal-Constitution to Quit Print Cold Turkey

text: An article on Monday about a pro-Palestinian demonstration that took place during the 82nd Venice International Film Festival on Saturday misstated the death toll cited by the Gaza Health Ministry. The ministry recently put the figure at more than 60,000 people, not over 39,000.
match_headline: Pro-Palestinian Protesters Raise Gaza War at the Venice Film Festival

text: A picture caption with an article on Sunday about healthy gardening misidentified two types of plants. The blue one is Love-in-a-Mist, or Nigella damascena, not fennel flower, and the red plant is Cardinal flower, or Lobelia cardinalis, not Lobelia tupa.
match_headline: The Era of the American Lawn Is Over

text: An article on Aug. 28 about Australia’s accusation that Iran directed an attack on a Jewish restaurant in Sydney referred incorrectly to statements made at a pro-Palestine rally at the Sydney Opera House on Oct. 9, 2023. Local officials concluded that some “offensive and completely unacceptable” statements against Jews were made; they did not conclude that no such statements were made.
match_headline: Bungling Arsonists, Antisemitic Attacks and a Diplomatic Rift

text: An article on Aug. 29 about the mystery behind a missing painting misstated the century in which “Portrait of a Lady” was painted. It was the 18th century, not the 17th.
match_headline: Looted by Nazis, an 18th-Century Painting Resurfaces. But Not for Long.

text: An article on Thursday about the Off Broadway show “The Brothers Size” misidentified a character that André Holland played in the production. He was Elegba, not Oshoosi.
match_headline: An Off Broadway Homecoming for Tarell Alvin McCraney and André Holland

text: An obituary on Saturday about the civil rights lawyer James E. Ferguson II misstated when he retired from the law firm Ferguson, Chambers & Sumter. It was in 2024, not 2014.
match_headline: James E. Ferguson II, Rights Lawyer Who Defended Busing, Dies at 82

text: An article on Friday about a U.S. warning that Lebanon’s leaders are running out of time to disarm the militant group Hezbollah gave an outdated title and affiliation for Firas Maksad. He is the managing director for the Middle East and North Africa at Eurasia Group, not an associate fellow at the Middle East Institute.
match_headline: Time Is Running Out for Lebanon to Disarm Hezbollah, U.S. Officials Warn

text: An article on Thursday about how a new generation of animal activists has used extreme tactics and viral video to challenge farmers in Sonoma County, a region in California known for some of the industry’s most humane practices, misidentified the number of years that Zoe Rosenberg faces in jail if she is convicted of the nighttime raid at Petaluma Poultry. She faces up to four and a half years, not six.
match_headline: A California County Embraces Humane Farming. These Animal Activists Demand More.

text: An article on Friday about the arraignment of a man accused of killing two young employees of the Israeli Embassy in Washington in May gave an incorrect title for Benjamin Netanyahu. He is the prime minister of Israel, not the president.
match_headline: Man Accused of Killing Israeli Embassy Staffers Pleads Not Guilty

text: An article on Sept. 1 about what to watch on television this week misidentified the MTV Video Music Awards statuette. It is a moon person, not a moon man.
match_headline: ‘The Paper,’ Plus 7 Things to Watch on TV this Week

text: An article this weekend on Page 18 about cheeseburgers misstates the name of an East Village park. It is Tompkins Square Park, not Thompson Square Park.
match_headline: I Can’t Get Enough of These Easy Cheeseburgers

text: An article on Saturday about the debate over an unproven link between acetaminophen, the active ingredient in the painkiller Tylenol, causing problems in children like autism and A.D.H.D. misstated the title of Brian Lee of Drexel University, the lead author of the study. He is a professor of epidemiology, not an associate professor.
match_headline: Debate Flares Over an Unproven Link Between Tylenol and Autism

text: An article on Sunday about the hundreds of L.G.B.T.Q. Catholics who participated in the church’s quarter-centennial festival of renewal and forgiveness on Saturday misstated the Catholic Church’s teaching on homosexual acts. The church considers them “intrinsically disordered,” not “intrinsically distorted.”
match_headline: L.G.B.T.Q. Catholics Have Jubilee With Pope’s Blessing, if Not His Presence

text: A headline with an article on Aug. 30 about concerns over Emil Bove’s continued work at the Justice Department after his confirmation as an appeals court judge misstated how the code of conduct for federal judges applies to him. As the article correctly noted, Mr. Bove has yet to be sworn in, and is therefore not bound by the code. It is not the case that he has disregarded it.
match_headline: Emil Bove Continued to Work at Justice Dept. After Judicial Confirmation

text: An article on Friday about the newly restored film “Linda Linda Linda” misspelled the band name in the movie. The group is Paranmaum, not Parlan Maum.
match_headline: ‘Linda Linda Linda’: These Girls Just Want to Have Fun

text: A news analysis article on Monday about the ways the Trump administration has undercut the legislative branch misstated the year in which the Foreign Malign Influence Center, created by Congress to counter election interference efforts, played a major role warning about Russian propaganda in a presidential campaign. It was 2024, not 2020.
match_headline: Trump Tramples Congress’s Power, With Little Challenge From G.O.P.

text: An article on Tuesday about the Trump administration’s announcement that it had begun a crackdown on illegal immigration in Chicago on Monday misidentified the organization where Tricia McLaughlin works. She is the assistant secretary for public affairs at the Department of Homeland Security, not Immigration and Customs Enforcement.
match_headline: Trump Administration Says It Has Begun Immigration Crackdown in Chicago

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday about the season debuts of N.F.L. quarterbacks who changed teams in the past year misstated, in one instance, the age of the Pittsburgh Steelers quarterback Aaron Rodgers. He is 41, not 42.

An article on Sunday about the Atlantic Coast leopard frog referred imprecisely to the team of biologists that identified the species. They were affiliated with several universities, not solely Rutgers University.
match_headline: The Endangered Frog That Lives Next to an Amazon Warehouse

text: An article on Sunday about the return of the remains of 42 people found outside a now-abandoned ethnic Polish village in western Ukraine after World War II misstated the location of the mass graves. They are in western Ukraine, not western Poland.
match_headline: A World War II Dispute Between Ukraine and Poland Is Put to Rest

text: An article on Wednesday about the search for a man suspected of killing a married couple in Queens and setting their house on fire, relying on information from the police, misstated the ages of the victims. The man was 76 and the woman 77, not 77 and 78.
match_headline: Suspect in Couple’s Fiery Deaths Went Door to Door Asking to Come In

text: An article on Thursday about the delay in repatriation of hundreds of South Korean workers arrested in an immigration raid in the United States last week, using information from a lawyer, misstated the nationalities of his clients. They included people from Colombia, Japan and Mexico, not only those from South Korea.
match_headline: ​Anger Mounts in Korea as Release of Workers Detained in Georgia Is Delayed

text: An article on Tuesday about the ways that neurologists are exploring medications that would help the brain recover after a stroke or traumatic injury misstated the professional title of Dr. Einor Ben Assayag. She is a geneticist, not a neurologist.
match_headline: A Pill to Heal the Brain Could Revolutionize Neuroscience

text: An article this weekend on Page 48 about the American photographer and memoirist Sally Mann misstates the year of Mann’s forthcoming show at Gagosian Gallery. It is planned for 2027, not 2026.
match_headline: Sally Mann, in Her Golden Hour, Faces Fresh Culture Wars

text: An article this weekend on Page 44 about “Monuments,” a landmark exhibition at the Geffen Contemporary at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Los Angeles that combines decommissioned statues of Confederate and other figures with works by 18 contemporary artists, misspells the name of an opera singer featured in a film that will be shown at the exhibition. He is Davóne Tines, not Tynes.
match_headline: Kara Walker Deconstructs a Statue, and a Myth

text: An article this weekend on Page 26 about JPMorgan’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein misstates the chronology of Mr. Epstein’s early career. He was a high school math teacher before working on Wall Street, not after.
match_headline: How JPMorgan Enabled the Crimes of Jeffrey Epstein

text: An obituary on Friday about the activist Charlie Kirk misidentified the beauty pageant that his wife won. It was Miss Arizona USA, not Miss Arizona.
match_headline: Charlie Kirk, Right-Wing Force and a Close Trump Ally, Dies at 31

text: An article on Sunday about Secretary of State Marco Rubio traveling to Israel over the weekend to consult with Israeli officials about their planned military offensive in Gaza City misstated the length of the war in Gaza. It began nearly two years ago, not nearly three years.
match_headline: Rubio Heads to a Defiant Israel After Qatar Strike

text: A Critic’s Notebook article on Friday about the conceptual artist and musician Stephen Prina misstated the age at which Mr. Prina scored a Mozart string quartet. He was 21, not 22.
match_headline: An Artist’s Do-Over in Double Time

text: An obituary on Sept. 10 about the internationally renowned conductor Christoph von Dohnanyi referred incorrectly to Mr. Dohnanyi’s appointment as chief conductor of the Frankfurt Opera in Germany. He took the helm in 1968, several years after Georg Solti stepped down from that position; it was not the case that he directly succeeded Solti. Two other conductors — Lovro von Matacic and Theodore Bloomfield — preceded Mr. Dohnanyi after Solti’s departure.
match_headline: Christoph von Dohnanyi, Conductor With a World of Admirers, Dies at 95

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about reactions from pharmaceutical companies and prominent investors to proposed anti-China measures misstated when Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick made comments about several types of medicines that are believed to have substantial production in China in a CNBC interview. The interview was on Sept. 5, a week earlier, not Sept. 12, the day before.
match_headline: The New York Times News Quiz, Sept. 5, 2025

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about efforts to protect workers from the dangers of heat stress misstated the Fahrenheit conversion of 31 degrees Celsius. It is 87.8 degrees, not 107 degrees.
match_headline: Extreme Heat Spurs New Laws Aimed at Protecting Workers Worldwide

text: An article on Saturday about acute necrotizing encephalopathy, a neurological condition that can result from the flu or other infections, misstated the date on which Olivia Yanxi Li, the 4-year-old who developed A.N.E., was sent home from school with a fever. It was Jan. 23, not Jan. 21.
match_headline: Rare but Serious Complication Shows Flu’s Potential Harm

text: An article on Sept. 7 about Jonathan Kleisner’s career transition from commodities trader to paramedic misidentified the country from which an 11-year-old girl and her family — all of whom died in the fire at the Twin Parks North West residential tower in the Bronx — emigrated. It was Gambia, not Guyana.
match_headline: The Millionaire Who Left Wall Street to Become a Paramedic

text: An article on Wednesday about Tim Walz’s announcement that he is seeking a third term as governor of Minnesota included an outdated estimate of Minnesota’s potential budget deficit. State budget officials in July projected that Minnesota would face a shortfall in 2028 of $1.1 billion, not nearly $6 billion, as the officials had projected earlier in the year before lawmakers made cuts.
match_headline: Tim Walz Seeks Third Term as Governor of Minnesota

text: An article on Sunday about the increase in political discourse among online hobbyists misspelled the surname of the host of The Guitologist channel on YouTube. He is Brad Linzy, not Lindzy.
match_headline: Guitar Nerds and Surf Bros Didn’t Want to ‘Get Political.’ Then Came the Tariffs.

text: An obituary on Saturday about the sculptor Robert Grosvenor misstated the size of a solar telescope that he said inspired his first major piece. It was 110 feet high, not 30. The error was repeated in a picture caption.
match_headline: Robert Grosvenor, Sculptor Who Challenged Gravity, Dies at 88

text: An article on Friday about a man from Texas who was charged with terroristic threat and aggravated harassment against Zohran Mamdani misstated the name of the Texas county where the man was held in jail before being brought to New York to face charges. It is Collin, not Collins.
match_headline: Texas Man Is Charged With Making Threats Against Mamdani

text: An article on Thursday about a shooting in southern Pennsylvania in which three police officers were killed referred incorrectly to the families with whom Governor Shapiro met on Wednesday. They were the families of the officers who were injured in the shooting, not those of the officers who were killed.
match_headline: 3 Police Officers Are Killed in Shooting in Southern Pennsylvania

text: A picture caption with an article on Thursday about the royal welcome that Britain extended to President Trump on a recent visit referred incorrectly to Charles. He is, of course, the king, not a prince.
match_headline: The Quiet Triumph of King Charles III

text: An article on Sunday about the Russian drone incursion in Poland misspelled the surname of a Polish Defense Ministry official. He is Pawel Zalewski, not Zalevski.
match_headline: A Polish Soldier, an Unusual Radar Dot and Then NATO Jets

text: An article on Sunday about new restrictions imposing by the Pentagon on reporters covering the Department of Defense misstated the number of press briefings Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has held. He has held a handful of briefings, not one.
match_headline: Pentagon Expands Its Restrictions on Reporter Access

text: An article on Sunday about the future of the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program misstated the name of an Illinois state department. It is the Department of Human Services, not the Department of Human Resources.
match_headline: States Grapple With Fundamental Change in Food Stamps

text: An article on Sunday about Pipeline Fund, a left-leaning group that is vying to establish itself as a liberal counter to the sprawling down-ballot infrastructure on the right that has helped Republicans notch wins in local races, using information from the Pipeline Fund, misstated the one of the states the group is expanding to. It is Massachusetts, not Connecticut.
match_headline: The Obscure Liberal Group Seeking to Curb Republican Down-Ballot Dominance

text: An article on Monday about Elon Musk redoubling his efforts with xAI since his falling out with President Trump in June misspelled the surname of an xAI founder. He is Igor Babuschkin, not Babuskin or Babushkin.
match_headline: Since Leaving Washington, Elon Musk Has Been All In on His A.I. Company

text: An obituary on Saturday about Agnes Gund, an art collector and philanthropist who served as president of the Museum of Modern Art for over a decade, misstated Glenn D. Lowry’s association with the Museum of Modern Art. He stepped down as director this month, shortly before Ms. Gund died; he is not the current director. The obituary also referred incorrectly to Ms. Gund’s sale of a Roy Lichtenstein painting, “Mirror #5.” It sold for $3.8 million, her family said, not $3.1 million, and Ms. Gund donated the proceeds to 29 organizations that advocate for reproductive rights, not one such group.
match_headline: Agnes Gund, Who Oversaw a Major Expansion of MoMA, Dies at 87

text: An article on Tuesday about a judge’s ruling on Monday that permits a Danish energy company to restart work on Revolution Wind, a large wind farm off the coast of Rhode Island that had been abruptly halted last month by the Trump administration misspelled, in some instances, the surname of the judge who granted the injunction. He is Royce Lamberth, not Lambert.
match_headline: Judge Says Work on Wind Farm Off Rhode Island Can Proceed, for Now

text: An article on Tuesday about an underwater gas pipeline that had been rejected three times in New York misstated Ken Lovett’s title. He is the governor’s communications adviser for energy and environment, not for energy and efficiency.
match_headline: Proposed Gas Pipeline Tests New York’s Allegiance to Green Energy

text: An article on Sept. 19 about the Trump administration’s announcement on the launch of a civics education coalition that includes the Education Department and more than 40 partner groups, all of which are closely aligned with the president’s Make America Great Again movement, misstated the circumstances around the founding of the Center for Civic Education. It traces its roots to planning for the bicentennial of the U.S. Constitution, not the nation’s bicentennial.
match_headline: Trump Administration Announces New Civics Effort With MAGA-Aligned Groups

text: An article on Sept. 17 about Sotheby’s acquisition of a Leonard Lauder collection valued at more than $400 million that will be auctioned in November misidentified the year that “Blooming Meadow” was created, in one instance. It was 1906, not 1908.
match_headline: Sotheby’s Lands a Leonard Lauder Art Collection Worth More Than $400 Million

text: An article on Wednesday about a new exhibition in Italy spotlighting the Renaissance painter Fra Angelico misidentified the religious community in which Angelico he served. He entered an order of Dominican friars, not monks.
match_headline: Reuniting the Great Works of the Patron Saint of Artists

text: An article on Wednesday about Lindsey Halligan, the lawyer chosen by President Trump to replace the ousted U.S. attorney in the Eastern District of Virginia, misidentified the group for which Ms. Halligan once interned for a semester in 2013. She worked at the Miami Innocence Clinic, which specializes in fighting wrongful convictions and is affiliated with the Innocence Network, not at the Innocence Project.
match_headline: Trump’s Pick to Replace Ousted U.S. Attorney Lacks Prosecutorial Experience

text: An article on Wednesday about the death of Abdulaziz Al Asheikh, Saudi Arabia’s most senior cleric, misstated Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s role in a group prayer in Riyadh held in honor of the grand mufti who died on Tuesday. He participated in a funeral prayer, he did not lead it.
match_headline: Saudi Grand Mufti, an Archconservative in a Changing Kingdom, Dies

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Friday about the U.S. Customs and Border Protection’s announcement regarding the detention of bicycles, parts, and accessories manufactured in Taiwan by Giant Manufacturing Co. Ltd., following an investigation that found evidence of forced labor, incorrectly described Walmart’s relationship with Giant. The retailer does not sell Giant bikes.
match_headline: Giant Brand Bikes Made in Taiwan Barred by Trump Administration

text: An article this weekend on Page 6 about the opening of Gladys Books & Wine in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn, misstates the age of Tiffany Dockery, the shop’s owner. She is 37 years old, not 39.
match_headline: A Shop for Black Women Who Love Women Who Love Books

text: An article on Aug. 31 about protests over debris from the Palisades and Eaton fires being taken to the Calabasas, Calif., landfill misidentified Kendra Wilkinson. She appeared as a model in Playboy magazine; she was not a Playmate of the Month.
match_headline: How a Ritzy L.A. Enclave Learned a Bitter Lesson About the Limits of Its Wealth

text: An obituary on Sept. 17 about the actor Robert Redford referred incorrectly to his tenure at the University of Colorado. He did not attend on a baseball scholarship.
match_headline: Robert Redford, Screen Idol Turned Director and Activist, Dies at 89

text: An article on Monday about the uncertainty federal workers are facing with a possible government shutdown this week misstated the name of a liberal think tank focused on inequality. It is the Washington Center for Equitable Growth, not the Washington Center for Equitable Health.
