text: An article on Thursday about public health advocates warning of the shortcomings of a U.N. declaration on chronic diseases mistakenly included the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States among the industry groups that issued statements praising the process and outcome of the U.N. declaration. The organization has not commented on the final document.
match_headline: U.N. Health Goals Weakened by Industry Pressure, Experts Say

text: A picture caption with an article on Saturday about Cleveland Browns defensive tackle Shelby Harris’s penchant for blocking field goal attempts described incorrectly the action in one of the photographs. While one image showed Harris celebrating after blocking a kick against the Green Bay Packers on Sept. 21, the other image was of him blocking a kick for another team in 2020; it was not from the Browns-Packers game.

An obituary on Friday about Sara Jane Moore, who attempted to assassinate President Gerald R. Ford in 1975, misspelled the surname of Stephen Sondheim’s collaborator on the musical “Assassins.” He is John Weidman, not Wideman.
match_headline: Sara Jane Moore, Would-Be Assassin of President Ford, Dies at 95

text: An obituary on Monday about Russell M. Nelson, the oldest serving president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, misstated the year that Mr. Nelson was appointed to the Quorum of Twelve Apostles, the Latter-day Saints’ second-highest governing body. It was 1984, not 1985.
match_headline: Russell Nelson, Who Led Major Changes to the Mormon Church, Dies at 101

text: An article on Sept. 8 about Chloë Bass’s new audio-based public art project that will be heard over the P.A. system at 14 M.T.A. stations around New York attributed an erroneous distinction to the languages spoken in New York City that were used in subway announcements. They are six of the top 10 languages spoken in the city, but not the top six.
match_headline: Those New Announcements in the Subway? They’re Art

text: A headline with an article on Oct. 1 about President Trump’s initiative to lower drug prices misstated the federal health insurance program that would be affected. The president’s plan involves Medicaid, not Medicare.
match_headline: How Trump’s Online Drugstore May Affect Your Drug Costs

text: An obituary on Thursday about the primatologist Jane Goodall misstated the number of chapters the Jane Goodall Institute has around the world — it has 25, not 34 — and it misstated the number of countries where it sponsors its Roots and Shoots program, which teaches young people about conservation. The program is provided in 75 countries, not 120. The obituary also referred incorrectly to Dr. Goodall’s public speaking in her last decades. She had traveled some 300 days a year on speaking tours, her institute said; she did not make 300 speaking appearances a year.
match_headline: Jane Goodall, Who Chronicled the Social Lives of Chimps, Dies at 91

text: An article on Thursday about the partial collapse of a Bronx building on Wednesday misstated the estimated cost of repairs and improvements needed in the New York City public housing system over the next 20 years. It is $80 billion, not $80 million.
match_headline: 20-Story Public Housing Building in the Bronx Partly Collapses

text: An article on Monday about a new museum in Mexico City showcasing the artist Frida Kahlo’s family and origins misidentified the substance that was used on Kahlo in her surgeries. It was chloroform, not formaldehyde.
match_headline: Frida Kahlo’s Prequel: A New Museum Shows Her Family Roots

text: An article on Tuesday about Lionel Richie’s memoir “Truly” misstated the previous name of Tuskegee University. It was Tuskegee Institute, not Tuskegee College.
match_headline: For Lionel Richie, Writing a Memoir Was Easy Like Sunday Morning. Sort Of.

text: An obituary on Wednesday about George Hardy, a retired Air Force lieutenant colonel who was one of the last surviving combat veterans of the Tuskegee Airmen, referred incorrectly to one of his sons, Gerald Hardy. He died a few years ago in an automobile accident; he does not survive his father.
match_headline: George Hardy, Tuskegee Airman Who Fought in Three Wars, Dies at 100

text: An article on Sept. 28 about the family business involvement of Steve Witkoff in the Middle East misstated the source of the Qatari government’s investments in think tanks, universities and lobbying groups. Those expenditures came from other organizations also backed by the Qatari government, not from the country’s sovereign wealth fund.
match_headline: Where Mideast Envoy Pitched Peace, His Son Pitched Investors

text: A news analysis article on Friday about the rising concerns of antisemitism in Britain following the Manchester synagogue attack on Thursday, using information provided by the police, misstated the number of people injured. It was three, not four.
match_headline: For Britain’s Jews, a New and Deadly Sign of Rising Antisemitism

text: An article on Friday about American comics at Saudi Arabia’s first global comedy festival misstated the title of a podcast on which Jim Jeffries had appeared before the festival. It is “This Past Weekend,” not “This Last Weekend.”
match_headline: At Saudi Comedy Fest, American Free Speech Becomes the Punchline

text: An article on Friday about the Iraqi-born artist Hayv Kahraman’s New York show “Ghost Fires” referred incorrectly to Kahraman’s paintings in her new show. They each have individual names. They don’t share a single title called ‘Untitled (To Be Titled).’”
match_headline: Forced to Flee Two Homes, a Painter Finds a Refuge on Canvas

text: An article on Tuesday about the shutdown of a North Carolina program that provided nutritious food, safe housing and transportation for doctors’ visits that helped Medicaid recipients misstated the number of people enrolled in North Carolina’s Medicaid pilot program and misattributed the source of the research estimating the pilot’s savings. While there were 13,000 people participating from the state’s western region, there were about 40,000 Medicaid recipients enrolled statewide. The analysis of savings was conducted by a research center at the University of North Carolina, not its medical school.
match_headline: Fragile N.C. Residents Lose Medicaid Support for Food and Housing

text: An article on Saturday about the scene inside Heaton Park Hebrew Congregation’s synagogue in Manchester, England, during the terrorist attack last Thursday, relying on initial reports from the police that were later updated, misstated the role of Melvin Cravitz, who died in Thursday’s attack. He was a worshiper at the synagogue, not a security guard, and he was outside the building, not inside. (The error was repeated in a separate article on Saturday about the man believed to have carried out the attack.)
match_headline: Shouts and Pounding at the Doors: How a U.K. Synagogue Came Under Attack

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Saturday about the Trump administration’s emphasis on defense concerns instead of climate research in the rapidly warming Arctic region misspelled the name of a United States Coast Guard ship. It is Healy, not Healey.
match_headline: In the Arctic, the U.S. Shifts Focus From Climate Research to Security

text: A picture caption in an article on Monday about popular destinations and their status during the government shutdown misstated the public access to Joshua Tree National Park in California. It remains open, not closed, according to the park’s website.
match_headline: Not All National Parks Remain Open in the Shutdown. Here’s What to Know.

text: A Critic’s Notebook article on Saturday about a new retrospective of Lee Miller’s work at Tate Britain in London misstated when Carolyn Burke first met Miller. They met in 1977, not 1970.
match_headline: The 9 Lives of Lee Miller

text: An obituary on Sunday about Phyllis Gardner, who as a pharmacology professor at Stanford told Elizabeth Holmes that her idea for a new blood-testing technology would fail, and later became a critical source in the investigation that led to Ms. Holmes’s conviction in one of the biggest fraud cases in U.S. history, misstated the relationship of Richard Fuisz to Ms. Holmes, the founder of Theranos. He was a friend of Holmes’s family, but was not also an early investor in the company.
match_headline: Phyllis Gardner, Early Skeptic of Theranos, Dies at 75

text: An article on Monday about dissidents in Nicaragua misstated the surname of a political prisoner whose body was returned to his family. He is Mauricio Alonso Petri, not Mauricio A. Petri.
match_headline: A Chilling New Tactic in Nicaragua: Arrest, Then Silence

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday explaining the purpose of the National Guard referred incorrectly to the police violence against Rodney King that led to riots in Los Angeles in 1992. He was beaten, not killed.
match_headline: What Is the National Guard?

text: An article on Tuesday about some arts institutions that are distancing themselves from the Ghanaian architect David Adjaye following accusations of sexual misconduct against Adjaye referred incorrectly to the status of a cathedral project in Ghana. The Ghanaian government is canceling the construction contract and ending the use of public funds to pay for it; it is not canceling the project. A final decision on its future awaits an audit.
match_headline: A Star Architect’s Buildings Soar. He’s Nowhere to Be Seen.

text: An article on Wednesday about lessons learned from the 2020 protests and riots in Portland following the murder of George Floyd misstated the given name of the assistant chief with the Portland Police Bureau who was the incident commander for many of the 2020 protests. He is Craig Dobson, not Chris Dobson.
match_headline: In 2020, Trump Intervened in Portland’s Protests. They Got Even Worse

text: An obituary on Thursday about Joan Kennedy, an accomplished pianist who married into one of America’s foremost political dynasties, misspelled the middle name of Ms. Kennedy’s father. He was Harry Wiggin Bennett Jr., not Wiggins.
match_headline: Joan Kennedy, Who Married Into a Dynasty, Is Dead at 89

text: An article on Friday about the recipient of the Nobel Prize of Literature, Hungarian novelist Laszlo Krasznahorkai, misidentified which work of his consisted of a single sentence. It is his novel “Herscht 07769,” not “The Melancholy of Resistance.” (This error also appeared in an August 2014 article .)
match_headline: Laszlo Krasznahorkai Is Awarded Nobel Prize in Literature

text: An article on Wednesday about government disputes over extending expiring health care subsidies, a chart overstated the cost of insurance in the Affordable Care Act marketplace and the value of certain subsidies from 2021 to 2025. The total cost of the premium for 2025 is $8,326, not $9,638.
match_headline: What’s Behind the Dispute Over Extending Health Care Subsidies

text: An article this weekend on Page 6 about the popular Twitch streamer Kai Cenat misstates the pronunciation of Cenat’s surname. It is seh-NAT, not seh-NOT. The article also misidentifies the location of his nonstop livestream last month. It was in Beverly Hills, not the Hollywood Hills neighborhood of Los Angeles.
match_headline: Kai Cenat Lets the Rizz Flow on His Streams

text: An obituary on Tuesday about Darleane Hoffman, an innovator in nuclear chemistry, referred incorrectly to two elements on the periodic table of the elements. A transuranium element studied by Albert Ghiorso of the Lawrence Berkeley Lab in 1974 has an atomic number of 106; its atomic weight is not 106. And uranium, a so-called heavy element, has a weight of 238, not 92, which is its atomic number. The obituary also described a transuranium element incorrectly. It refers to any element with an atomic number above 92 on the periodic table of the elements, not an atomic weight above 92.
match_headline: Darleane Hoffman, Innovator in Nuclear Chemistry, Dies at 98

text: An article this weekend on Page 6 about the popular Twitch streamer Kai Cenat misstates the pronunciation of Cenat’s surname. It is seh-NAT, not seh-NOT. The article also misidentifies the location of his nonstop livestream last month. It was in Beverly Hills, not the Hollywood Hills neighborhood of Los Angeles.

An obituary on Sunday about the actress Diane Keaton misstated Nancy Meyers’s role in the movie “Baby Boom,” in which Ms. Keaton starred. She wrote it but did not direct it. A picture caption with a reprint of the obituary on Monday referred incorrectly to Ms. Keaton’s Oscar nomination for her performance in the 2003 comedy “Something’s Gotta Give.” It was her fourth nomination for best actress, not her third. (Her third was for the 1996 drama “Marvin’s Room.” )
match_headline: Diane Keaton, a Star of ‘Annie Hall’ and ‘First Wives Club,’ Dies at 79

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about the way a Harvard protest over the Israel-Hamas war has become a touchstone for broader debate over campus unrest misstated the background of a student. He is South Asian, not Southeast Asian.
match_headline: The Harvard ‘Die-in’ That Set Off a Debate Over Protest and Punishment

text: An article on Sunday about the Sunday routine for Anjula Acharia, who hosts one of the most coveted Diwali balls in New York City every year, misstated the name of Ms. Acharia’s record label. It is 5 Junction, not 5 Junctures.
match_headline: How the Host of a Diwali Ball Spends Her Day Getting Ready to Party

text: A picture caption with an obituary on Sunday about John Lodge, the singer and bassist for the British rock group the Moody Blues, misspelled the given name of a member of the band. He was Graeme Edge, not Graham.
match_headline: John Lodge, Singer and Bassist With the Moody Blues, Dies at 82

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday about an N.B.A. player’s adjustment to living in Minnesota and playing for the Timberwolves after they acquired him in a trade with the New York Knicks misspelled his surname in some instances. He is Julius Randle, not Randall.
match_headline: New York Confirms State’s First Locally Acquired Case of Chikungunya

text: An article on Tuesday about New York City Ballet’s fashion gala omitted the first name of a dancer with that company. The dancer is Taylor Stanley.
match_headline: Honeycomb Wings and Jellyfish: These Costumes Dance, Too

text: An obituary on Sept. 21 about Jack Daniels, a two-time Olympic medalist in the modern pentathlon and an exercise physiologist, misstated the given name of Daniels’ father. He was Ronald Daniels, not Robert (though he was known informally as Bob).
match_headline: Jack Daniels, Olympian and ‘World’s Best’ Running Coach, Is Dead at 92

text: An article on Wednesday about the naming of Dallin H. Oaks as the next president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints misstated the age of D. Todd Christofferson, a counselor who will join Mr. Oaks in the First Presidency. He is 80, not 79.
match_headline: Dallin H. Oaks Named President of the Mormon Church

text: An article on Wednesday about the impact of France’s political turmoil on its economy misstated the title of Olivier Redoulès at Rexecode, a French consultancy. He is an economist there, not the founder.
match_headline: Political Turmoil in France Weighs on Businesses and Investors

text: A picture caption with an article on Monday about the cease-fire deal in Gaza misstated a central component of the agreement. As the article correctly noted, the deal involved the release of the Israeli hostages in Gaza and the release of Palestinian prisoners and detainees held by Israel; it was not a hostage exchange.
match_headline: ‘Over the Clouds’: Families of Palestinian Prisoners Await Their Release

text: An article on Friday about a growing number of high-ranking Democrats from New York who are urging Gov. Kathy Hochul to reject a plan for an underwater gas pipeline off New York City misstated the deadline by which the Department of Environmental Conservation must decide the fate of the pipeline project. The deadline is the end of November, not next spring.
match_headline: N.Y. Democrats Urge Hochul to Reject Pipeline Over Climate Concerns

text: An article this weekend on Page 16 about current investment and regulatory strategies and their similarities to the speculative excesses that preceded the 1929 crash misstates the requirements for quarterly financial statements filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Such statements must be reviewed by an independent accountant; they do not need to be independently audited.
match_headline: The Rules of Investing Are Being Loosened. Could It Lead to the Next 1929?

text: An article this weekend on Page 22 about New York City mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani misidentifies the office held by Jabari Brisport. He is a state senator, not a state assemblyman.
match_headline: Inside the Improbable, Audacious and (So Far) Unstoppable Rise of Zohran Mamdani

text: An article this weekend on Page 14 about the arranging and dismantling of one of the world’s largest and most complete stegosaurus skeletons misstates Melissa Posen’s title at the American Museum of Natural History. She is the vice president of exhibitions, not the senior director of exhibition operations.
match_headline: One of the World’s Largest Stegosaurus Skeletons Was Moved. Twice.

text: An article this weekend on Page 28 about U.S. museums and their recruitment of young people to curate their shows this fall misspells the name of a student curator. It is lk’ayqn Moomaw, not Lkayan Moomaw.
match_headline: One Way to Shake Up Museum Curation? Hand the Keys to the Kids.

text: A news analysis article on Sunday about the lessons from the Richard Nixon era in relation to President Trump’s use of impoundment power misstated the year in which the Supreme Court case Train v. City of New York was decided. The ruling was made in 1975, not 1985.
match_headline: There Are Lessons From the Nixon Era in Trump’s Attempts to Freeze Spending

text: An article on Monday about the government shutdown’s effects on the operations of the federal court system, starting Oct. 20, referred incorrectly to the fund the judiciary drew on to keep operating after the shutdown. The fund comprised case filing fees and leftover appropriations from previous years, not fees the public pays to gain access to court records online.
match_headline: Funding Runs Out for Federal Courts, Threatening Delays and Staff Shortages

text: An article on Sunday about the actress Glenn Close misstated Close’s familial relationship to C.W. Post. He was the father of Close’s grandfather’s first wife; she is not related by blood to Post. The article referred incorrectly to Close’s living situation in New York in the 1970s. A guy she once dated used a board over the bathtub as a dining table; Close never dined that way in her own apartment. The article also misstated where a neighbor was once seen typing naked in the window; it was at Close’s boyfriend’s apartment, not her own.
match_headline: Glenn Close Has Nothing Left to Prove

text: An article on Sunday about a handful of artist-led groups using data to explore the climate crisis misstated the name of a London-based artist studio. It is ScanLAB Projects, not ScanLAB.
match_headline: Art and Data Team Up Against Climate Change

text: An article on Sunday about the Portland Art Museum’s $116 million renovation and expansion misstated the name of one of the architectural firms that designed the Mark Rothko Pavilion, the 21,000-square-foot glass structure anchoring the museum. It is Vinci Hamp, not VinciHamp.
match_headline: Bringing the Portland Art Museum Back to Life

text: An obituary on Monday about the physicist Chen Ning Yang misspelled the name of the German mathematician who developed gauge theory. He was Hermann Weyl, not Herman Wyl.
match_headline: Chen Ning Yang, Nobel-Winning Physicist, Is Dead at 103

text: An article on Friday about the C.I.A.’s involvement in Latin America misstated the year Rafael Leónidas Trujillo Molina, the authoritarian leader of the Dominican Republic, was killed. It was 1961, not 1975.
match_headline: Poison Cigars, Propaganda and Coups Litter C.I.A. History in Latin America

text: An article on Saturday about three new cases of monkeypox in California misstated the name of the pharmaceutical company that makes a vaccine for protection against the infection. Jynneos is the name of the vaccine. The manufacturer is Bavarian Nordic.
match_headline: Three Cases of Mpox Tied to Severe Illness Worry Health Experts

text: An article on Monday about the reactions of Jewish New Yorkers after the return of Israeli hostages last week misidentified the member of Leon Goldenberg’s family who was assaulted. The victim was his grandson, not his nephew.
match_headline: Relief and Grief: What Jewish New Yorkers Feel After the Hostage Release

text: An article on Tuesday about two reported encounters between surfers and sea otters in Santa Cruz, Calif., misstated when the incidents occurred. It was last week, not this week.
match_headline: Sea Otters Are Stealing Surfboards in California. Again.

text: A picture caption with an article on Monday about the deportation of a young woman and her mother to Guatemala misidentified the young woman shown dressed for her quinceanera. It was the cousin of Nory Sontay Ramos, not Sontay Ramos.
match_headline: Nory Doesn’t Go to School Here Anymore

text: An article on Monday about Bari Weiss’s first days as the editor of CBS News misstated the air date of a “60 Minutes” episode featuring interviews with Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff, the architects of President Trump’s Middle East peace plan. It was last Sunday, not this Sunday.
match_headline: Booking Big Guests and Irked by Leaks: Bari Weiss’s First Days at CBS

text: An article on Tuesday about a new musical adaptation of Prince’s 1984 film “Purple Rain” misstated Prince’s involvement with the music club First Avenue. He played there, but he never owned the club.
match_headline: Prince’s ‘Purple Rain’ Takes the Stage in Minneapolis

text: An article accompanying the obituary about Ace Frehley on Saturday misattributed a quote to Frehley that appeared in a Kiss interview with Rolling Stone. It was Peter Criss who said, “I see myself eventually on my own without the makeup and the bombs, without theatrics. I could dig getting up there with a white suit and three chick singers.”
ref_headline: I see myself eventually on my own without the makeup and the bombs, without theatrics. I could dig getting up there with a white suit and three chick singers.
match_headline: How Ace Frehley Made ‘New York Groove’ His Own

text: An obituary on Tuesday about the writer Alison Rose misstated where the offices of The New Yorker were located when she began working there as a receptionist. They were on West 43rd Street in Manhattan, not West 45th Street.
match_headline: Alison Rose, The New Yorker’s Femme Fatale, Dies at 81

text: An article on Tuesday about political debates over the investment in artificial intelligence in Chile misstated the expected timing of the government’s final plan for data center development in northern Chile. Officials said their hope is to announce the plan by the end of the year, not before national elections next month.
match_headline: How Chile Embodies A.I.’s No-Win Politics

text: An article on Wednesday about six heist film recommendations incorrectly attributed a fake sculpture of Venus that figured in the movie “How to Steal a Million.” It was the grandfather of Audrey Hepburn’s character, not her father, who created it.
match_headline: Watch These Six Heist Movies

text: An article on Oct. 15 about the connection between a U.S.-based group that opposes abortion rights and Britain’s ascendant Reform U.K. party referred incorrectly to the 2025 Reform U.K. party conference. It was Nigel Farage’s second conference as leader, not his first.
match_headline: They Helped Topple Roe v. Wade. Now Their Sights Are Set on Britain.

text: An article on Tuesday about a study that shows how food allergies in children dropped sharply in the years after new guidelines encouraged parents to introduce infants to peanut products referred imprecisely to the recommendations on the introduction of possible food allergens to infants. Studies recommended introducing a small amount of peanut products, not peanuts, to infants. (Whole peanuts could pose a choking hazard.)
match_headline: Peanut Allergies Have Plummeted in Children, Study Shows

text: An article on Thursday about President Trump’s acknowledgment on Wednesday that he was having the entire East Wing demolished to make way for a 90,000-square-foot ballroom included a comment from Sara C. Bronin, a law professor at George Washington University, in which she misstated the function of the National Historic Preservation Act. It is not the case that the demolition of the East Wing may run afoul of the act; in fact, the act contains an exception for White House projects.
match_headline: Trump Is Wasting No Time in Tearing Down the East Wing

text: An article on Thursday about a report’s findings that deforestation happening to expand coffee crops is imperiling the long-term prospects of coffee crops misstated the findings on precipitation in the Amazon. Deforestation has driven about 75 percent of the decrease in rain there, it did not drive a 75 percent drop in rain.
match_headline: Coffee Production May Be Imperiled as Forests Are Destroyed for More Crops

text: An article on Thursday about the Trump administration’s cuts to cyberdefense as threats have grown misstated the name of the agency whose size had been cut by a third. It is the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, not the Cyberspace and Infrastructure Security Agency.
match_headline: Trump Administration Cuts Cyberdefense Even as Threats Grow

text: An article on Friday about King Charles III and Pope Leo XIV praying together in an ecumenical service at the Sistine Chapel on Thursday, which was the first time in hundreds of years that a pontiff and a British monarch have publicly prayed together, misidentified the way in which King Henry VIII chose to dissolve his first of several marriages. The king sought an annulment and not a divorce.
match_headline: King Charles and Pope Leo Pray Together in Groundbreaking Moment

text: An article on Friday about several measures to pay essential government employees that were blocked in the Senate on Thursday referred incorrectly to the U.S. Postal Service employees’ status in government shutdowns. The Postal Service is separately funded, and its workers continue to receive paychecks; they do not work without pay.
match_headline: Democrats Block Federal Worker Pay Bill as Shutdown Drags On

text: An article this weekend on Page 8 about Vince Gilligan’s new television series, “Pluribus,” included erroneous information from Sony, which misstates the length of Gilligan’s overall production deal with Sony Pictures Television. It is over 15 years, not over 20.
match_headline: ‘Pluribus’ Is a Singular Vision of a Tedious Utopia

text: An article this weekend on Page 14 about the director Kathryn Bigelow misstates the number of women who have won the best director Oscar since Bigelow’s historic win. It is two, not one.
match_headline: Kathryn Bigelow Returns to the Intersection of Facts and Thrills

text: An article on Page 13 this weekend about the marketing campaign for Taylor Swift’s album “The Life of a Showgirl” misstates the date when orange doors, an element of the campaign, made appearances in cities around the world. It was Saturday, Oct. 4, not Oct. 5. And the article misstates the title of a movie released as part of the campaign. It was “The Official Release Party of a Showgirl,” not “The Release Party of a Showgirl.”
match_headline: Taylor Swift Is Our Biggest Cinematic Universe. But the Magic Is Fading.

text: An article on Monday about the impact that President Trump’s policies have had on Iowans included an outdated figure for the change in Iowa’s economic output in the first quarter of 2025. According to a revised estimate by the Bureau of Economic Analysis, it contracted at a 1.2 percent annual rate in the quarter; the bureau’s initial estimate was a “6.1 percent contraction.”
match_headline: In Trump-Friendly Iowa, the President’s Policies Have Hit Hard

text: An article on Sunday about the expectations for President Trump’s nearly weeklong tour of Asia misstated the last time Mr. Trump made an official visit to Southeast Asia. It was 2019, not 2017.
match_headline: Where Trump Is Headed in Asia, and What Each Country Wants From Him

text: An article on Friday about a marathon speech on the Senate floor by Jeff Merkley, an Oregon Democrat, misstated when the chamber was to vote on legislation to clear payments for some government workers. The vote took place this past Thursday. It is not the case that it was expected this coming Thursday.
match_headline: Trump Said to Demand Justice Dept. Pay Him $230 Million for Past Cases

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption in an article on Friday about the history of the East Wing at the White House misstated a location on the White House grounds in an image from 1962. Jacqueline Kennedy was showing Empress Farah, wife of the Shah of Iran, the White House grounds near the Oval Office, not the East Colonnade.
match_headline: A Look Back at the White House East Wing

text: An article on Saturday about the Justice Department’s case against New York’s attorney general, Letitia James, as an illustration of President Trump’s influence on the department misstated the date on which Ms. James was to appear in a Virginia court. She pleaded not guilty on Friday to one count of bank fraud and one count of making a false statement to a financial institution. It is not the case that she had yet to appear in court.
match_headline: Letitia James Case Shows Ruthlessness of Justice Dept. in Trump’s Grip

text: An obituary on Monday about the actress June Lockhart misstated the name of one of her daughters. She is June Elizabeth Lockhart-Triolo, not June Elizabeth Trola.
match_headline: June Lockhart, Beloved Television Mother, Dies at 100

text: Because of an editing error, a news analysis article on Sunday about Speaker Mike Johnson’s decision to put the House on an indefinite hiatus misstated the constitutional powers of the House. It has the sole power to originate tax legislation, not spending legislation.
match_headline: Keeping the House Absent, Johnson Marginalizes Congress and Himself

text: An article on Tuesday about Nick Mangold, the former All-Pro center for the Jets who died this week, misstated the number of this season’s Super Bowl. The title game, which will be held in February, will be Super Bowl LX, not Super Bowl XL.
match_headline: The Odd History of the Rowhouse at the Center of the N.B.A. Betting Scandal

text: A reporter’s notebook article on Tuesday about the nu metal scene misstated the chronology of Korn’s albums. “Follow the Leader” was the band’s third album, not its sophomore album.
match_headline: Y2K Kids Were the Last to Really Rage With No One Watching

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Thursday about President Trump’s desire to help broker peace between North and South Korea misidentified which gift Mr. Trump joked about wanting to wear. It was a medal, not a crown. The error was repeated in a separate article about South Korea’s agreement on a trade deal with the United States
match_headline: Feted as a Peacemaker in South Korea, Trump Finds Purpose for Overtures to Kim Jong-un

text: An article on Monday about the drone manufacturer Fire Point, one of the largest contractors for the Ukrainian military, misstated what action the Public Anticorruption Council called for in response to quality and pricing concerns. The council has considered calling for a parliamentary inquiry but has not yet done so.
match_headline: 3 Years Ago It Was a Casting Agency. Now It Has $1 Billion in Drone Contracts.

text: An article on Thursday about Radio Free Asia shutting down its news operations for the first time since its founding in 1996 misstated the position of Cameron Lang at the broadcaster. She is the associate general counsel at Radio Free Asia, not the general counsel.
match_headline: Radio Free Asia Will Halt News Operations Amid Shutdown

text: An article on Oct. 26 about the history of the movie theater that was located in the East Wing of the White House misstated the timeline of John F. Kennedy’s viewing of “From Russia With Love” in the theater. He watched it on Oct. 23, 1963, but not a second time on Nov. 21.
match_headline: Among the East Wing Rubble: The Presidency’s ‘Best Perk’

text: An article on Friday about how candy companies are quietly cutting down on cocoa because of climate change included an outdated title for John Satumba. He is now vice president for global food innovation at Cargill, not director of global edible oil solutions research and development.
match_headline: What’s Missing From Your Favorite Chocolate Bar? It May Be Chocolate.

text: An article this weekend on Page 4 about the 50th anniversary season of Steppenwolf, one of America’s pre-eminent theater companies, misstated the date “Bug” will begin previews. It is Dec. 16, not Dec. 17.
match_headline: 50 Years of ‘Freedom Onstage’ at Steppenwolf

text: An article on Sept. 7 about A.I. in art misspelled the surname of a writer and an artist who wrote a book about A.I. and art. His name is Jan Svenungsson, not Svennungsen.
match_headline: A.I. Enters the Museum

text: An article on Page 20 this weekend about the comedian Stavros Halkias misstates the title of his half-hour, crowd-work-only special. It is “Four Nights in NYC,” not “Live at the Lodge Room.”
match_headline: Stavros Halkias Wants a Career. Will His Fans Let Him Have One?

text: An article on Saturday about fact-checking President Trump’s claim about SNAP funding and partisanship misstated the year of a survey by the Pew Research Center that measured the percentages of Republicans and Democrats who said they had received food stamps. It was in 2012, not 2013.
match_headline: Fact-Checking Trump’s Claim About SNAP and Partisanship

text: An article on Sunday about former President Barack Obama calling New York City mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani on Saturday misstated the number of mayoral candidates endorsed by Mr. Obama since he left office. He has publicly endorsed two other mayoral candidates, not one.
match_headline: Obama Calls Mamdani to Praise His Campaign and Offers to Be Sounding Board

text: An article on Jan. 22 about the Ghanaian musician Ebo Taylor, using information provided by a publicist, misstated Taylor’s age at the time of publication. He was 89, not 90. The error was repeated in the headline.
match_headline: At 89, the Ghanaian Highlife Pioneer Ebo Taylor Finds a New Voice

text: An article on Oct. 28 about Saudi Arabia’s artificial intelligence ambitions in the geopolitical tussle for tech power misstated the relationship between Aramco, the Saudi state oil giant, and DeepSeek. Aramco is deploying DeepSeek open-source A.I. technology at one of its data centers. DeepSeek is not using Aramco data centers.
match_headline: Saudi Arabia’s New Power Play Is Exporting A.I. to the World

text: An article on Saturday about the best autumn road trips misstated the century in which Paso Robles, Calif., became a hot springs destination as well as home to a large concentration of almond orchards. It was the 19th century, not the 18th.
match_headline: Soak Up Fall Splendor on These 5 Western Road Trips

text: An article on Sep. 4 about Kathryn Bigelow’s new thriller, “A House of Dynamite,” misattributed a line in the movie. It was Jared Harris’s character, the secretary of defense, who said, “That’s what $50 billion buys us?,” not Tracy Letts’s character, a general in STRATCOM.
match_headline: At Venice, ‘A House of Dynamite’ Is Scarier Than Most Horror Films

text: An article on Oct. 28 about Ken Maracle and his handmade wampum belts misstated the year that the “Wampum:Beads of Diplomacy” exhibition was displayed at the McCord Stewart Museum in Montreal. It was 2023, not 2013.
match_headline: Enslaved Potter’s Art, Displayed at Boston Museum, Returns to Heirs at Last

text: An article on Wednesday about religious organizations struggling to feed New Yorkers during the SNAP stalemate referred imprecisely to SNAP statistics. Of those relying on SNAP for food, 39 percent were children, 20 percent were seniors and 10 percent were people with disabilities. It is not the case that 39 percent of children, 20 percent of seniors and 10 percent of disabled people across the country depend on SNAP.
match_headline: Houses of Worship Struggle to Feed New Yorkers During SNAP Stalemate

text: An article on Wednesday about Vanderbilt University establishing a satellite campus at the General Theological Seminary in New York City referred incorrectly to the Master of Divinity program at the seminary. It is currently among the largest Master of Divinity programs in an Episcopal seminary in the United States. It is not the largest priest training program in the Episcopal Church in the country.
match_headline: Aging N.Y.C. Seminary’s Prayers Are Answered With a Lease by Vanderbilt

text: An article on Sunday about how Carbondale, a college town in Illinois’s southern tip, has become a major abortion hub inaccurately described legal access to abortion in Missouri. Access is severely restricted in the state, not banned.
match_headline: A Small Illinois City at the Center of a Seismic Shift in Abortion Access

text: An obituary on Saturday about Cardinal Dominik Duka, a former Archbishop of Prague, referred incorrectly to Cardinal Duka’s offer to resign when he reached 75, the mandatory retirement age for a Catholic bishop. He offered to resign as archbishop , not as a cardinal. (He remained a cardinal at his death.) The obituary also misstated the number of years that Pope Francis kept him on as archbishop after that point. It was four years, not two.
match_headline: Dominik Duka, Czech Cardinal Jailed Under Communism, Dies at 82

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Wednesday about this year’s best genre movies misidentified the breed of the dog who stars in “Good Boy.” He is a Nova Scotia duck tolling retriever, not a golden retriever.
match_headline: The Best Genre Movies of 2025

text: An article on Wednesday about Philadelphia’s efforts to deter crime with environmental design referred imprecisely to enhancements made to Philadelphia’s streetlight network. While community efforts helped shape the enhancements as the project proceeded, the streetlight project was already underway.
match_headline: In Philadelphia, Beautified Blocks Build Community and Safety

text: Because of an editing error, an article on Thursday about Jared Isaacman, the private astronaut and billionaire entrepreneur, being confirmed on Wednesday to lead NASA misspelled the first name of a senator from Washington. She is Maria Cantwell, not Marie.
match_headline: Trump’s NASA Pick Is Confirmed to Lead Space Program in Peril

text: An article on Thursday about the rarity of lengthy manhunts in high-profile attacks like the recent Brown University shooting has misstated how one of the police officers died after the Boston Marathon bombing. He was wounded by a bomb blast, not gunfire.
match_headline: One Way the Brown Attack Was Unusual: The Gunman Escaped

text: An obituary on Friday about the war correspondent Peter Arnett misstated the title of a CNN documentary he anchored in 1998. It was “Valley of Death,” not “Operation Tailwind.” (Operation Tailwind was the name of a U.S. covert military mission in Laos in 1970, the subject of the broadcast.)
match_headline: Peter Arnett, Pulitzer-Winning War Correspondent, Dies at 91

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption accompanying an article on Sunday about international transplant patients misstated the source of a donation to a foundation. The donation was made by a charity run by the hotel magnate Ryuko Hira, not by Mr. Hira himself.
match_headline: Hospitals Cater to ‘Transplant Tourists’ as U.S. Patients Wait for Organs

text: An obituary on Dec. 16 about the actor and director Rob Reiner referred incorrectly to Kris Perry. She was the second executive director of the California commission charged with overseeing the disbursement of funds from tobacco taxes for early childhood education, now known as First 5 California, not the first. Jane Henderson was the commission’s first executive director.
match_headline: Rob Reiner, Actor Who Went on to Direct Classic Films, Dies at 78

text: A news analysis article on Sunday about how President Trump has greatly expanded executive power while embracing the trappings of royalty in ways not seen in the modern era misstated the name of a British royal family residence. It is Windsor Castle, not Windsor Palace.
match_headline: Trump Takes America’s ‘Imperial Presidency’ to a New Level

text: An article on Tuesday about the Trump administration announcement that it would pause leases for five wind farms under construction off the east coast misspelled the name of the New Bedford mayor. He is Jon Mitchell, not John Mitchell. Because of an editing error, the article also misstated the approval the five wind farms’ received from the Biden administration. They received permits, not leases.
match_headline: Trump Halts 5 Wind Farms Off the East Coast

text: An article on Tuesday about Scott C. Beardsley being appointed president at the University of Virginia misstated the total number of students at the university. It has more than 26,000 students, not more than 40,000.
match_headline: University of Virginia’s Board Names a New President

text: A critic’s notebook article on Tuesday about two South Korean drama thrillers to stream misidentified an actor in the Hulu series “Nine Puzzles.” The detective character Han-saem was played by Son Seok-koo; Robert M. Lee voiced the character in the English dub.
match_headline: In These K-Dramas, Women Are Unjustly Accused (Maybe)

text: An article on Monday about how Google has quietly started to roll out a way for users to change their Gmail addresses ending with @gmail.com and keep all their emails, no matter how old their account, misidentified which part of a Gmail account Google has allowed users to change for years. It is the sender name, not the username.
match_headline: Gmail to Let Users Change Their Addresses While Keeping Data

text: An article on Tuesday about some of the new A.I. billionaires misstated the age of Michael Truell, a co-founder of Cursor. He is 25, not 24. It also misstated the academic status of Mr. Truell and his Cursor co-founders, Sualeh Asif, Aman Sanger and Arvid Lunnemark, at M.I.T. They graduated in 2022; they did not drop out that year.
match_headline: The New Billionaires of the A.I. Boom

text: Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Saturday about memorable images of the year in culture misstated the location of a 100-year-old equestrian statue of Stonewall Jackson. It was in Charlottesville, Va., not Charlottesville, N.C.
